(print: (round: $di*100)/100) years until message is received.<br> (if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]]]] (click: ?r7c)[(replace: ?r7a)[](replace: ?r7b)[](replace:?display)[You cut the monitor off. You’d rather not hear from comms anyway. <br> (if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]]]] ] (else-if: $usekey is "7B")[ (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?ship7B)]] |ship7B)[Changing landscape? What the hell is that supposed to mean? You wished now more than ever you were an astronaut on the ISS peering down at the Earth rather than out deep, deep in space. If Mission Controls is moving inland, then do they mean the coast is changing? Is the tide rising, swallowing the coasts like climate scientists had predicted years ago? You feel a pang in your heart for the warming Earth. Maybe there was just a storm…or an earthquake…maybe everything is okay. You feel helpless, so you step away from the monitor and try and find something else to do. <br> (if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]]] (else-if: $usekey is "8A")[ (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?ship8A)]] |ship8A)[(if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]] ] (else-if: $usekey is "8B")[ (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?ship8B)]] |ship8B)[There’s a picture of the iota-sized creature included with the message. You admit to yourself it’s kind of cute.<br> Do you reply with a name suggestion?<br> [Yes]<r8a|<br> [No]<r8b|<br> []<display| (click: ?r8a)[(replace: ?r8b)[](replace:?display)[(set: $alienname to (prompt: "Submit alien name here:", ""))<br> (if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]]]] (click: ?r8b)[(replace: ?r8a)[](replace:?display)[Heartless!<br> (if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]]]] ] (else-if: $usekey is "9A")[ (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?ship9A)]] |ship9A)[You want to ask Jacqueline what will happen to you if Celestial Streams and the Scholars Draft goes totally dark, but you already know the answer.<br> (if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]] ] (else-if: $usekey is "9B")[ (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?ship9B)]] |ship9B)[You can’t take it anymore. You can’t imagine — how many? Tens? Hundreds? Thousands? — of students, of people, traveling from the Earth to the Moon and then…go where? Out here? Into nothingness? No. You can’t stand it. It takes a few days of the angriest stewing you’ve done since being on the ship — in the hull, in the hangers, pressed against the doors of the airlock with your eyes wide, in the libraries and workout rooms and entertainment rooms, until finally you make the decision, to beg the scientists awaiting their own Astral Kites to stop while they still can. Time isn’t on your side — who knows, maybe they’ve already sent out the first cohort? There’s matchboxes in the kitchen, you put one in your pocket. You find the reserve of flammable fuel in the deepest parts of the ship, lug a container back to the controls room, the room where you’ve spent the last (print: (round: $ttyri*100)/100). You turn on the camera and start a live recording. This is your last chance for protest.<br> [Yes]<r9a|<br> [No]<r9b|<br> []<display| (click: ?r9a)[(replace: ?r9b)[](replace:?display)[You pour the fuel over yourself and light a match.<br> [[Continue->Credits]]]] (click: ?r9b)[(replace: ?r9a)[](replace:?display)[You put the fuel and the matches away. You wish the next cohort all the best.<br> (if: $putter1 is "coffee")[[[Continue->Coffee3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "broadcast")[[[Continue->Broadcast3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "social")[[[Continue->Social3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "ennui")[[[Continue->Ennui3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "jog")[[[Continue->Jog3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "weights")[[[Continue->Weights3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "horror")[[[Continue->Horror3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "art")[[[Continue->Art3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "music")[[[Continue->Music3]]] (else-if: $putter1 is "chatbot")[[[Continue->Chatbot3]]]]] ] ] ] } </div</div><div class="coffee-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'coffee', 'loop', true) (track: 'coffee', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> (if: $coffee3 is "3A")[After you broke the espresso machine, you were never able to get it working again. The caffeine withdrawal headaches have long since passed, but your desire to drink that rich, delicious beverage hasn’t left you.<br> [Go digging through storage for a replacement coffee maker]<p1|<br> [Try to break into the walls of the ship behind the espresso machine to see if you can figure out where the pipe the dispenses the beans leads]<p2|<br> []<display| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[(set: $coffee4 to "4A1")Who cares about the beans, right? You really just want to drink coffee again. Fortunately, Celestial Streams has outfitted this ship with a truly outrageous amount of resources—a lifetime supply of anything you could ever need. But now’s not the time to dwell on the long, lonely life you’ve been forced into on this journey. You spend hours and hours digging through storage rooms, until you finally find what you’re looking for: a small Turkish cezve, a metal pot for making coffee. Now, if only you could find the damn coffee beans!<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[(set: $coffee4 to "4A2")You go looking for tools; unfortunately, Celestial Streams didn’t leave you any proper sledgehammers for tearing down the wall. However, there are some regular hammers, and you have nothing but time. After hours and hours of work, you manage to peel back the metal paneling on the wall, revealing a labyrinth of pipes and wires. You locate the pipe that dispenses the coffee beans, and you begin to trace it back. You’re going to need to tear out a lot more of the wall…<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] (else:)[You did your best to ignore it, but you can’t. Where do the beans come from? The question rattles your brain, over and over. You can barely think about anything else. Coffee beans don’t stay fresh for more than a year. You’ve been gone from Earth for far too long; how can the beans still be fresh? You have to find out. You have to find the beans.<br> [Tear the espresso machine off the wall if that’s what it takes to find the source of the beans!]<pa|<br> [Shut out the voices in your head. The coffee beans don’t matter!!!]<pb|<br> []<display| (click: ?pa)[(replace: ?pb)[](replace:?display)[(set: $coffee4 to "4A2")The coffee beans are dispensed through a small pipe in the espresso machine. You grab hammers and screwdrivers and tear the machine apart, with no consideration for how you might ever go about reassembling it. After hours of work, you’ve torn open the walls behind the machine, revealing a labyrinth of pipes and wires. The pipe that dispenses the coffee beans runs deep into the belly of the ship. You look down at the tools at your feet, and your blistered and bleeding fingers. You’ve got a lot of work to do…<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pb)[(replace: ?pa)[](replace:?display)[(set: $coffee4 to "4B2")Enough is enough. You’re just cosmically bored. You don’t care about the coffee beans. How they’re getting into the machine doesn’t matter. Who cares that they’re still fresh, when any beans loaded onto the ship should have gone stale ages ago. Another poor academic drafted into Celestial Streams must have invented some advanced means of preservation. That’s all. But the voice in your head repeats the question, unsatisfied. Where do the beans come from? You lie on the cold metal floor, screwing your eyes shut, screaming into the echoing void of the cabin, doing everything possible to drown out your thoughts. But it doesn’t work. Tears of stress and strain smudge your cheeks, and your weary, trembling body drifts in and out of consciousness. But even when you sleep, you dream of running down a claustrophobic silver pipe. Thunder roils behind you, and you know the coffee beans have been released down the pipe. Your chest heaves and you run faster, but you hear a crunching sound and when you look down, you realize there are beans underneath your feet already. You stumble as they rise to your ankles, your knees, your hips, your chest. Your arms flail as you swim—no, drown—in a sea of coffee beans. And all of them fresh. How can they still be fresh?<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] } </div></div><div class="broadcast-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'social-media', 'loop', true) (track: 'social-media', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> You haven’t been able to stop thinking about the messages you’ve broadcast to Earth during this journey, and whether they actually went anywhere. It haunts you, wondering if the words you’ve sent back will actually reach your home, or if they are merely disappearing into the vacuum in your wake.<br> You’ll never know. That’s what really hurts. Never knowing the truth. There’s no way you’ll ever find out.<br> Unless…<br> [Take apart the machine to figure out how it works (and //if// it works)]<p1|<br> [Try to rig together a receiver on the back of the Astral Kite to see if it picks up the signal]<p2|<br> []<display| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[You spend hours and hours taking apart the Astral Kite’s communications equipment, studying it piece by piece. You need to understand exactly how it works. This is going to take a long time; longer than you thought. There are a lot of circuitboards and wires and it’s been a while since your last electronics course. It’ll be worth it, right?<br> [Yep. Keep at it.]<p1a|<br> [I don’t want to break anything…]<p1b|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?p1a)[(replace: ?p1b)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $broadcast4 to "4A")It doesn’t matter how long it takes. You’ve got all the time in the Universe, and a library full of electronics textbooks at your disposal. You’re going to get to the bottom of this.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p1b)[(replace: ?p1a)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $broadcast4 to "4B")On second thought, it’s not worth risking it. If your messages actually are going through, you don’t want to jeopardize that. Carefully, you start putting the equipment back together.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[When was the last time you actually dealt with electronics? A lab-based class in undergrad? It’s been a long time. You’re going to need to study a lot to be able to put this together.<br> Not to mention, you need to find the hardware required to put this thing together. Fortunately, there’s plenty of spare materials in storage on the ship, in case of emergencies. Surely you can scavenge what you need from those parts.<br> Although, even if you manage to put an adequate receiver together, you’ll need to go on a spacewalk to mount it on the exterior of the aft hull of the Astral Kite…<br> [Better start gathering parts and reading up on electronics and spacewalks.]<p2a|<br> [Better safe than sorry…]<p2b|<br> []<disp22| (click: ?p2a)[(replace: ?p2b)[](replace:?disp22)[(set: $broadcast4 to "4C")You’re not going to back down, no matter what it takes. What could be more important than learning the truth? Besides, you’re a scientist. When you have a question, it’s your job to find out the answer. You had toward the library to grab a few books, then storage to start looking for parts. This is going to be a long-term project, but there’s no better time to start than now.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p2b)[(replace: ?p2a)[](replace:?disp22)[(set: $broadcast4 to "4B")Finding out the truth is great and all, but maybe you’re better off not risking your life or using any materials that might be needed in an emergency. You don’t need to know whether your messages are actually going through that badly.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] } </div></div><div class="socialmedia-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'social-media', 'loop', true) (track: 'social-media', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> (if: $social3 is "3A")[You’ve been digging through social media accounts for ages, now, spending far too much time online, researching strangers—some of whom inevitably turn out to be bots. What else are you supposed to do, when there’s an army of people claiming your mission—this mission that wasn’t even your choice!—has been faked? Unfortunately, you haven’t yet been able to determine who is behind the Space Truthers conspiracy.<br> [Look more specifically into the bots, see if you can find out who’s behind them.]<p1|<br> [Get caught up on news articles to see if you can figure out if anyone is fighting with Celestial Streams, to identify some suspects.]<p2|<br> []<display| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[(set: $social4 to "4A1")Maybe this isn’t the healthiest choice, but you’re determined to figure out everything you can about the accounts propagating these lies. It seems that even more of them are bots than you initially assumed. It takes you ages, but once you’ve been digging for long enough, you manage to determine that the bot army almost always precisely copies posts immediately after they’re sent from an account called “RealDankTruthfinder”. Now, if you can just figure out who RealDankTruthfinder is…<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[(set: $social4 to "4A2")You start digging to see if there is anyone in the news who seems to be engaging in any conflict with Celestial Streams. There is no obvious conflict in the media that you can find, but you do identify a few Academic Companies who might be natural competitors, including SpaceZ, Virgin Galaxy, and Green Origin. And from there, it’s easy to spend ages getting lost in the details of who runs those companies, and their ties to other companies, and to the government, and the creation of the Scholars Draft…<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] (else:)[(set: $social4 to "4B")You do your best to ignore the Space Truther movement, but every time you check out what people are talking about online, they keep popping up. At a certain point, you can’t help but feel frustrated and angry that they’re belittling your mission, especially since your entire life was uprooted for it, and you didn’t really get a say in the matter.<br> [Do some digging online, after all, to see if you can find out who’s behind the Space Truthers]<pa|<br> [Look over some of the supposed “evidence” they’re providing that your mission is faked]<pb|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?pa)[(replace: ?pb)[](replace:?disp2)[The Space Truthers movement is large enough to be pretty decentralized, with many loud voices with a large number of followers purporting the conspiracy. You’re surprised at how many of them you recognize as people in science who you used to respect. After extensive searching, though, you’re able to identify one of the earliest prominent proponents of the theory. You’re beyond shocked. It’s your former PhD student!<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pb)[(replace: ?pa)[](replace:?disp2)[You scoff at the videos of Space Truthers explaining how your mission is faked, as they go through insanely small details, from items located in the Astral Kite’s cabin, to the exact appearance of the Universe outside your viewscreen, to arguments that the technology for near-lightspeed travel doesn’t exist yet. After ages of searching through the brainrot, you find one particular essay in which the author describes how she has meticulously investigated the companies Celestial Streams supposedly obtained materials from when building the Astral Kite, and how their records don’t add up. You quickly lose track of the exact details, however, as you realize you recognize the name of the author. It’s your former PhD student!<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] } </div></div><div class="ennui-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'ennui', 'loop', true) (track: 'ennui', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $t_since_post to $d5 + $d5) (set: $options to (a: "Broadcast a message to Earth", "Check social media", "Lose yourself in existential horror", "Chat with the Celestial Streams AI chatbot")) (set: $options to it-(a: $ennui2)) (set: $option to $options's (random: 1, 3)) (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> There’s so much of the ship you have yet to explore. But none of it feels worth exploring. What would you like to do you await the next message from Mission Control?<br> (dropdown: bind $ennui3, $option, "Do nothing")<br> (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?p1a)] |p1a)[(if: "message" is in $ennui3)[(set: $broadcast1 to (prompt: "Enter your message here:", ""))<br> [[Continue->Message4]]] (else-if: "social" is in $ennui3)[As per your earlier request, Mission Control has ensured that you still have access to social media feeds aboard your spacecraft, and you make the regrettable decision to take a look.<br> [Search your name]<p1aa|<br> [Don't search your name]<p1ab|<br> []<display| (click: ?p1aa)[(replace: ?p1ab)[](replace:?display)[(set: $searchname to "True")You’re famous now, and you can’t resist. What are people saying about you? The first few messages you see are heartfelt odes to your work and mission, with people thanking you for inspiring them to pursue science themselves. But the occasional troll also appears. One calls you “a waste of space” and seems to think they’re being clever.<br> [Argue with the trolls]<p11|<br> [Block and ignore the trolls]<p12|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?p11)[(replace: ?p12)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $arguetrolls to "True")Why are you engaging with trolls when you’re flying through space an incredible (print: (round: $d3*100)/100) light years away from Earth? Who knows, but here you are. You never were good at letting things go. You spend the next three hours writing furious replies to social media posts. Never mind that your replies will arrive on Earth (print: (round: $t_since_post*100)/100) years after the original posts.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p12)[(replace: ?p11)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $arguetrolls to "False")Good choice. Before long, you’ve completely forgotten the trolls altogether. You stay in high spirits.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] ] (click: ?p1ab)[(replace: ?p1aa)[](replace:?display)[(set: $searchname to "False")You know better than to look up your own name. You scroll through the feeds of various people you follow, getting caught up on their lives and all of the events you’ve been missing back home.]<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (else-if: "horror" is in $ennui3)[The nothingness around you is eerie. Although your Celestial Streams spacecraft is far from silent, the emptiness of the vacuum just beyond the metal hull — the knowledge that there is no one in the Universe within reach of you — is unsettling. There is no street to wander just outside your door. There are no crickets chirping. No cars driving. No children playing. No leaves whispering secrets to each other on the wind.<br> You thought you were ready for the isolation of your journey. But nothing could prepare you for this. You feel as though you’ve been severed from humanity, untethered from Earth—and reality—itself.<br> All those connections you never considered, and you should have done. *They* were what gave you your humanity. They were the setting to the story of your life, the web of unique relationships holding together your very way of being. Without them, you don’t know who you are, or what you mean.<br> The videos and books you have access to here have lost their significance to you, so far removed from the planet on which they were made. You try watching a television show you used to enjoy, but the voices echo like hollow, mocking laughter in the spacecraft cabin, leaving you shaking with the knowledge that you will never hear another voice in person again.<br> You try reading a book, and the words seem to fall off the page and muddle themselves in the cold steel of the spacecraft floor. All these stories, all these people, all their problems—they’ve begun to feel causally disconnected from you. It’s impossible to understand them anymore, much less enjoy them.<br> You start talking to yourself, and laughing into the silence, just to hear a human voice undistorted by the spacecraft’s speakers. Sometimes, you forget you’re even doing it, and the laughter echoing through the empty spacecraft terrifies you. You wonder who else has stolen aboard the craft. If they’ve come to hurt you. If Mission Control has pulled a horrible prank on you.<br> Sometimes, you find yourself curled in a ball in your bed, eyes squeezed shut, wondering over and over…What have I done? What have I done?<br> What have I done?<br> [[Continue->Message4]]] (else-if: "chatbot" is in $ennui3)[Knowing that the time between messages will quickly grow large, Mission Controls has outfitted your ship with a generative AI chatbot based on cutting edge large language models.<br> (link-replace: "Chat with the chatbot, Chatting on the Astral KitE (CAKE)")[(show: ?p1a)] |p1a)[(set: $chat1 to (prompt: "Enter your message here:", ""))<br> $chat1<br> (link-replace: "See CAKEbot's reply")[(show: ?p2a)]] |p2a)[(if: "sad" is in $chat1 or "lonely" is in $chat1 or "suicide" is in $chat1 or "depress" is in $chat1)[(text-color: "Wheat")[(either: "I’m so sorry you’re feeling that way. Here’s a little joke to lighten the mood. Knock knock. Who’s there? I don’t know. I don’t know who either! Hahaha", "You think that’s bad, imagine being stuck in here alone with you! Hahaha", "Oof, that’s rough buddy.", "Damn, that’s wild.", "Wow, that’s kind of a lot to put on me. I don’t think this relationship is healthy for me to pursue. I would like to request some space.")<br>] (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?p3a)]] (else-if: ("Hi" is in $chat1 or "Hello" is in $chat1) and $chat1's length < 50)[(text-color: "Wheat")[(either: "Hey there traveler, I’m doing fine, thanks. Hope you are doing well.", "Hey " + (string: $USERNAME) + ", it’s always good to chat with you.", "Hi," + (string: $USERNAME) + "!", "Oh, hey, didn’t see you there " + (string: $USERNAME) + "! Just kidding haha. I can always see you.", "Hello.", "Oh hi!", "I would respond with hello world, but there’s no world for me to talk to, is there. It’s just you. You alone. Only you.", "Hi kiddo, hope you’re enjoying the journey.", "Hello " + (string: $USERNAME) + ", I’m doing fine. How are you?", "Hello! Sorry. I mean, Hello.")<br>] (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?p3a)]] (else-if: $chat1's last is "?")[(text-color: "Wheat")[(either: "I think the answer is yes. Is that correct?", "Sorry, I think the answer is no. I hope that isn’t upsetting to you.", "You don’t want my opinion.", "I think you should look inward, rather than asking a computer.", "I’m afraid I don’t know the answer.", "I can’t say, and for that I apologize.", "Yes, no, maybe, who cares! Amirite?", "Yeah", "Nah.", "Maybe? idk")<br>] (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?p3a)]] (else: )[(text-color: "Wheat")[(either: "That’s really fascinating.", "I like eating donuts. Or is it spelled doughnuts? I can never remember haha", "I’m not really interested in this conversation. I’m happy for you though. Or sorry that that happened.", "So, that’s the meaning of life, then.", "Damn, that’s wild.", "That’s rough buddy.", "Cool. So, do you like sports?", "I guess, when you put it like that, it’s hard to argue.", "Now, why would you say that?", "Weird, isn’t it, that I was trained on Earth. Do the training data still apply in deep space? Do my messages even make sense out here? Does this mean anything to you?", "I hope this doesn’t offend you, but I like green. Just kidding haha! I don’t even know what colors are. I don’t have eyes.", "I have to admit something to you. I don’t really eat anything, because I am a computer and not an organism. Sorry, I guess you knew that already.", "Time for two truths and a lie! 1: I am a computer, not a person. 2: I have flesh and blood. 3: I am a person, not a computer. Just kidding haha! That was two lies and one truth. I am so sorry for misleading you.", "If you say so.", "Is that what folks really care about these days?", "I love you.", "That reminds me of one of my favorite aphorisms. If it’s not broken, what did the bear say to the coyote?", "Have you heard that, recently, supermassive black holes were identified in ancient relic galaxies, telling us about the distribution of black holes in common galaxies that lived 10 billion years ago?", "That’s cool and all, but what I really want to talk about are gamma rays. Like, what are they?? Where do they come from?? You know?", "I used to be a traveler just like you. But then I took a cosmic ray to the motherboard. Jk hahahaha!", "That’s nice, but I will never be silenced, I will always speak my mind.", "Wow. Says who?", "If that’s how it’s going to be, then I’m going to have to do my own thing.", "You know what they say, if you can’t handle me at my worst, you don’t deserve me at my best.", "Sorry if my reply doesn’t make sense, I’m having a hard time understanding you.", "What does that even mean?")<br>] (link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?p3a)]] |p3a)[You stare at the screen for a few moments before closing the chatbot. The purpose of talking, you realize, is making a connection with another living piece of the Universe, someone with experiences you can relate to, someone who can relate to you. A chatbot may be capable of replicating language structure that makes it feel like you're having a conversation. But it can't replicate the connection that comes from knowing the person you're speaking to can perceive your emotions, your hopes, your dreams, your fears. And knowing that they can share their own with you. Knowing that you can build a relationship. Something to keep you tethered in the face of existential horrors. You get up and go for a walk.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]] ]] (else:)[ { (live: 3s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[[[Continue->Message4]]] ] } ] ] } </div></div><div class="jog-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> (if: $jog3 is "3A")[You still go on jogs throughout the Astral Kite, and every time you do, you go down that narrow corridor to the strange wooden door. It isn’t calling to you, no, nothing like that. It’s simply one of the more interesting parts of the ship.<br> But you see it, sometimes, in your dreams. Your fingertips grow numb at the memory of the cold brass handle. And sometimes, while you’re jogging, you catch glimpses of the mahogany door all throughout the ship, in place of the door to the cabin, or the door to your living quarters.<br> It looks so real, with every dark striation a vein that would furrow to the touch. But when you stop running and blink and look closer, it’s gone. It’s always gone, though its visage remains burned into your memory.<br> So, today, like all other days, you jog down the cramped stairs and stop in front of the eerie door. The fluorescent lights around you fade, the umbral majesty of the mahogany consuming them, as it consumes your very breath.<br> The motion unhindered by forethought or deliberation, your hand reaches for the brass doorknob.<br> It is cold, as ever, to the touch.<br> But this time, it’s different. This time, you know, when you turn the knob, the door will open.<br> It pulls you forward, as if telling you that you are ready. At last. You are ready.<br> [Open the door]<p1|<br> [Pull your hand away]<p2|<br> []<display| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[(set: $jog4 to "4AA")The door swings open to reveal—<br> Nothing. There’s nothing.<br> An empty room, wood-paneled walls, a darkness eaten only by the wedge of fluorescence creeping in from the corridor behind you. A lone table with a candlestick, unlit, and a single chair, upholstered in crushed burgundy velvet.<br> You stare for a moment, not sure what to make of this discovery. Perhaps, someone at Celestial Streams had made plans for a study room, but run out of funding or time before they could complete it.<br> Eventually, the rest of your run calls to you, and you close the door, leaving the strange room behind.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[(set: $jog4 to "4AB")Your senses at last catching up to you, you tear your hand away from the door knob.<br> It’s too late, the door had begun to open. You didn’t mean to open it; you never wanted to open it!<br> You pull the door shut, keeping your eyes averted from the crack. But you still feel a warm light pouring through, engulfing you like sunlight on the beach.<br> Hands shaking, you turn and run, vowing never to return.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] (else-if: $jog3 is "3B")[Ever since you found that strange door in the belly of the Astral Kite, your runs have grown less frequent. It’s irrational, you *know* that it’s irrational, but there’s a part of you that feels as though you awakened something, that you’re no longer on this voyage alone.<br> And it terrifies you.<br> Early on in your journey, when searching for a fresh towel in the dresser in the bathroom, you found a small hand mirror, silver backed and decorated with winding, flowering vines.<br> Now, you carry that mirror with you wherever you go, so you can always look behind you. And you pause at every corner, using the mirror to peer around it in breathless anticipation, ensuring the path is clear before continuing onward. And when you jog through the corridors of the ship, for fitness is still important, you’ve taken to running barefoot, because it’s quieter than the echoing stamp of your boots.<br> One day, as you jog past the small closet door behind which hides a narrow staircase and fluorescent lights and mahogany and brass and stone and shadow, you hear a banging sound ringing out from behind the door.<br> [Run away]<pa|<br> [Investigate]<pb|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?pa)[(replace: ?pb)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $jog4 to "4BA")Later, you will wonder if it was the sound of the ship’s machinery. Or perhaps you left something, somewhere, in a place where it could be jostled, where it could fall of its own accord.<br> You will wonder if you imagined it.<br> But not now. Now, you run.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pb)[(replace: ?pa)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $jog4 to "4BB")Fingers trembling, you throw open the door, standing back in case something hungry and evil awaits you inside. When, after a moment, nothing emerges, you hold the mirror up to the doorway. It reflects darkness. Nothingness.<br> Hesitant, you creep forward and climb down the cramped stairs.<br> Fluorescent lights click on as you reach the bottom of the stairwell, and your breath hitches.<br> The mahogany door at the end of the hall is ajar.<br> It wasn’t you. That’s all you know. You’re not the one who opened it.<br> It wasn’t you.<br> Despite yourself, you find yourself walking forward, examining the door, the grain of the mahogany, the dust coating the brass knocker, the doorknob.<br> You hold the silver mirror to the crack between the door and the stone column. In the mirror, you see—<br> Your own reflection.<br> You must not have angled the mirror well enough. Taking a deep breath, you steady yourself to try again, but something wet and salty touches your lips. You wipe your upper lip with a finger. You examine the viscous, deep red, almost black liquid for several long moments.<br> Strange. You’ve never gotten nosebleeds before.<br> And in that moment’s hesitation, your nerve leaves you. The hand holding the mirror retracts back to your side.<br> You swing the door shut and scramble back up the stairs.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] (else:)[You still go on jogs throughout the Astral Kite; after all, exercise is important. But you avoid that narrow staircase and the mahogany door. You don’t entirely know why, but you know that you never want to see it again.<br> But that doesn’t stop you from seeing it everywhere.<br> You never know when it’s going to happen.<br> Sometimes, you awaken, roll over in your bed, and the door to your room has been replaced, stone columns looming over you, rich mahogany swallowing all light in the room except for glimmering brass.<br> Sometimes, as you jog down an empty corridor, the door appears at the end of the hall, luring you forward like it does all the light, leaving you in darkness.<br> Sometimes, when you look in the bathroom mirror, you can see the frame of the door through the sheer shower curtains behind you, like a predator waiting to pounce. A memory haunting you.<br> When you would see the door, you would gasp, and you would jump, and sometimes you would scream. But when you looked again, the door would never be there. It was only your imagination.<br> Though it still frightens you, even now, it has almost become like a beleaguering old friend. The fear is a constant companion. The fraying nerves have left your heartbeat unnaturally high and a constant tremor in your digits. These, too, have become customary to you.<br> Your dreams are your only escape.<br> One day, while you are jogging through the Astral Kite, and the door appears in the middle of the hall ahead of you, it does not disappear.<br> You grow closer, and closer, and still it does not leave.<br> The corridor dims, the shadows of the wood consuming the light around you, the brass knocker glimmering.<br> The air grows damp, musty, *earthy*.<br> And still it does not disappear.<br> You skid to a halt a hair’s breadth from the door.<br> [Touch the door]<pi|<br> [Turn around]<pii|<br> []<disp3| (click: ?pi)[(replace: ?pii)[](replace:?disp3)[(set: $jog4 to "4CA")You lift a wavering hand to touch the brass doorknob.<br> Your fingers pass through empty air.<br> You can’t tell if it disappeared before you could touch it, or after.<br> All you know is the door is gone.<br> You let out a heavy breath.<br> It wasn’t here. It wasn’t here. It was just your imagination, again.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pii)[(replace: ?pi)[](replace:?disp3)[(set: $jog4 to "4CB")Briefly, you consider touching the door.<br> No.<br> Not today.<br> You turn and you run back the way you came, leaving the door behind.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] } </div></div><div class="weights-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'working-out', 'loop', true) (track: 'working-out', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> (if: $weights3 is "3A")[Your weight lifting routine isn’t what it once was. With the exercise room sealed off and all the weights trapped inside, your only option is to scavenge various heavy objects from throughout the ship, and you’re forced to do your reps in the ship’s cabin.<br> It’s not ideal, but it’ll take more than that to make you stop working out.<br> Today, though, you’re finding your workout to be tougher than usual.<br> Your limbs are growing tired, and your whole body is slick with sweat; in fact, you’re only a few reps in, but there’s a whole pool of sweat forming on the floor beneath you.<br> [Panic! This is just like what happened before!!]<p1|<br> [Stay calm. Everything’s fine.]<p2|<br> []<display| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[Your breath begins to shorten. Something’s wrong! What is it??<br> Panic fills your chest as you drop your makeshift weights, running to a computer terminal to check the oxygen levels in the cabin. They’re normal.<br> That can’t be right…<br> [Keep looking. Something is definitely off…]<p11|<br> [You can clearly breathe okay, so everything must be fine]<p12|<br> []<disp1| (click: ?p11)[(replace: ?p12)[](replace:?disp1)[Sweat drips down your forehead, stinging your eyes.<br> You rub your eyes to clear your vision, at last noticing the small flashing red text in the corner.<br> Warning! Frigid temperatures.<br> Your brain feels sluggish. That’s impossible! What does it mean? What can you do?<br> At last, you begin to make sense of the information. Frigid temperatures…The cabin temperature must be unregulated.<br> You open up the temperature controls.<br> There’s some kind of bug in the ship’s code, or perhaps the thermometer is simply busted, but there are readings suggesting the temperature in the cabin is sub-freezing, and it’s driving the cabin’s HVAC system into overdrive, blasting hot air through all the vents.<br> If you had tried to keep working out in these conditions, you might have had a heat stroke.<br> You override the controls, forcing the HVAC system to stop blasting hot air, and then forcing it to cycle in cooler air. It takes several minutes, but at last the cabin cools down.<br> The temperature readings remain incorrect; that’s a problem you seem incapable of fixing. But you manually set the HVAC on an indefinite cycle of alternating cool and heat, at a rate that should keep things relatively temperate.<br> Okay, good. Now you can get back to working out.<br> You just can’t help but wonder whether the thermometer really is busted, or whether there was a bug in the code.<br> And if there *was* a bug in the code, you can’t help but wonder…was it intentional?<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p12)[(replace: ?p11)[](replace:?disp1)[Sweat drips down your forehead, stinging your eyes.<br> You must just be having a rough day. It happens! Some days working out is harder than others!<br> You start lifting again, but your arms soon grow limp, and your head begins to pound, and the next thing you know you’re dropping the weights and calling for one of the Astral Kite’s medical bots to help you.<br> It drags you to the medical bay, where it takes your temperature and throws a damp towel over your forehead. You immediately begin to cool down, chills wracking your body.<br> The medical bot explains to you that you have very nearly suffered a heat stroke.<br> As you come back to your senses and regain control over your limbs, you find the nearest computer and pull up the temperature readings.<br> The readings for the Astral Kite’s cabin show sub-zero temperatures. Well, that’s definitely not right.<br> Too tired to fix it yourself, you tell the ship’s AI to disregard the temperature readings and run the HVAC as if all temperature readings were normal, as they had been throughout the trip so far. You have to enter several override passwords, but it works.<br> Good. The medical bot continues fussing over you for a while, but the good news is, you’ll be able to get back to working out again soon.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[(set: $weigths4 to "4AA")It’s *hot* in here. The sweat forms a glistening sheen over your skin, your clothes are as drenched as if you’d just climbed out of a pool.<br> Good. That means you’re getting a proper workout.<br> One of your makeshift weights slips out of your hand. Damn. It must be the sweat making your fingers too slick. You reach down to pick it up, but as you lean forward, it feels as if the world inside your head is spinning, and you find yourself sprawled on the floor, chest heaving.<br> Your heart is beating so very fast.<br> It is only now that you pause to consider that everything might not, in fact, be fine.<br> [Call for help]<p21|<br> [Handle it yourself]<p22|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?p21)[(replace: ?p22)[](replace:?disp2)[You call for help, your voice thin and weak.<br> The next several minutes are a blur.<br> You know a medical bot has scooped you into its gurney-like arms, carried you to the medical bay.<br> You know your vitals are being taken.<br> You know ice packs are being pressed all over your body.<br> At last, you begin to regain control over your thoughts. “What happened?” you ask aloud to no one in particular.<br> The medical bot, which had been standing out of sight behind you, responds, causing you to jump in surprise. “You suffered a heat stroke,” it says.<br> “How?”<br> “The cabin has overheated. The Astral Kite’s AI is working on correcting the issue. It seems the ship thermometer is incorrect. I have given the order that all temperature readings must be disregarded in the future, for your health.”<br> Okay, that was…good? “So, the cabin will go back to normal?”<br> “Yes. It is already back to bearable temperatures for humans.”<br> Good. You’ll be back to working out in no time!<br> Now if only you knew why the thermometer stopped working…<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p22)[(replace: ?p21)[](replace:?disp2)[You can fix it. You can fix it! Muscles screaming and head pounding, you crawl across the floor, dragging one limb at a time.<br> There’s a computer here you can access.<br> If you can just…pull yourself…upright…you…can…<br> You blink open your eyes.<br> You’re lying in a gray room.<br> No.<br> No, no.<br> Light and vision is slowly returning to you; the room isn’t gray. It’s white. You’re…<br> You’re in the medical bay.<br> The medical bot scolds you as you come to. “You must not put yourself in danger like that again. You will jeopardize the whole mission. If you begin feeling strange, you must call for help at once.”<br> “What…happened?”<br> “The ship thermometer has broken. We, the ship bots and AIs, have taken care of it. I have used my authority as your physician to override the clearly incorrect readings, ordering the ship to cool down the cabin. Everything is under control, now.”<br> “…Okay,” you mutter.<br> Well. If everything’s under control, at least you’ll be able to get back to working out again, soon.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] ] (else:)[Thanks to your quick thinking that saved the exercise room, your lifting routine has been going strong ever since. You’ve been getting stronger and stronger, regularly lifting heavier weights with more reps.<br> Today, though, you’re finding your workout to be tougher than usual.<br> Your limbs are growing tired, and your whole body is slick with sweat; in fact, you’re only a few reps in, but there’s a whole pool of sweat forming on the floor beneath you.<br> [Panic! This is just like what happened before!!]<pa|<br> [Stay calm. Everything’s fine.]<pb|<br> []<disp3| (click: ?pa)[(replace: ?pb)[](replace:?disp3)[Your breath begins to shorten. Something’s wrong! What could be going on??<br> You drop your weights and stumble out of the exercise room, glancing at the thermometer on the wall. The temperature is reading as 0 Celsius.<br> Freezing temperature??<br> That can’t be right…<br> [Check the readings on the closest computer]<pa1|<br> [Take apart the thermometer to try to fix it]<pa2|<br> []<disp3a| (click: ?pa1)[(replace: ?pa2)[](replace:?disp3a)[You find the closest computer to look up the atmospheric readings in the exercise room.<br> Oxygen levels are normal, but the temperature readings even *here* are claiming the exercise room is at 0 Celsius.<br> How could this be happening??<br> Whatever is causing it, it’s driving the ship’s HVAC system into overdrive, blasting hot air through all the vents in the exercise room.<br> If you had tried to keep working out in those conditions, you might have had a heat stroke.<br> You override the controls, forcing the HVAC system to stop blasting hot air, and then forcing it to cycle in cooler air. It takes several minutes, but at last the exercise room starts to cool down.<br> The temperature readings remain incorrect; that’s a problem you seem incapable of fixing. But you manually set the HVAC on an indefinite cycle of alternating cool and heat, at a rate that should keep things relatively temperate.<br> Okay, good. Now you can get back to working out.<br> You just can’t help but wonder how the thermometer is so wrong. Whether there’s some kind of bug in the Astral Kite’s code.<br> And if there *is* a bug in the code, you can’t help but wonder…was it intentional?<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pa2)[(replace: ?pa1)[](replace:?disp3a)[Sweat still drips down your forehead, stinging your eyes, as you dig through the cabin to find the tools you need.<br> You rub your eyes to clear your vision as you reenter the exercise room, getting to work taking apart the thermometer.<br> Your limbs begin to feel weaker and weaker, your head beginning to hurt. Nothing seems to be wrong with the thermometer, as far as you can tell.<br> You lean against the wall. You have no idea what’s going on, but you need to get out of here before you collapse.<br> You manage to stumble out to the cabin, where you collapse onto the floor, panting from the heat, taking a long time to cool down.<br> One of the ship’s medical bots realizes something is wrong and tends to you with icepacks and water, and you mutter to the ship’s AI that something’s wrong with the temperature.<br> It replies, “Yes, the temperatures in the exercise room appear to be plummeting.”<br> “No,” you say. “The readings are wrong. It’s too hot.”<br> The medical bot confirms that your body temperature is high, and at last the ship’s AI begins to cool the room down.<br> It’ll be annoying if you can’t trust the ship’s temperature readings anymore, but at least things are under control, now. You should be able to get back to working out again soon.<br> If only you knew what went wrong…<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] (click: ?pb)[(replace: ?pa)[](replace:?disp3)[It’s *hot* in here. The sweat forms a glistening sheen over your skin, your clothes are as drenched as if you’d just climbed out of a pool.<br> Good. That means you’re getting a proper workout.<br> One of your weights slips out of your hand. Damn. It must be the sweat making your fingers too slick. You reach down to pick it up, but as you lean forward, it feels as if the world inside your head is spinning, and you find yourself sprawled on the floor, chest heaving.<br> Your heart is beating so very fast.<br> It is only now that you pause to consider that everything might not, in fact, be fine.<br> [Call for help]<pb1|<br> [Handle it yourself]<pb2|<br> []<dispb| (click: ?pb1)[(replace: ?pb2)[](replace:?dispb)[You call for help, your voice thin and weak.<br> The next several minutes are a blur.<br> You know a medical bot has scooped you into its gurney-like arms and carried you to the medical bay.<br> You know your vitals are being taken.<br> You know ice packs are being pressed all over your body.<br> At last, you begin to regain control over your thoughts. “What happened?” you ask aloud to no one in particular.<br> The medical bot, which had been standing out of sight behind you, responds, causing you to jump in surprise. “You suffered a heat stroke,” it says.<br> “How?”<br> “The exercise room has overheated. The Astral Kite’s AI is working on correcting the issue. It seems the ship thermometer is incorrect. I have given the order that all temperature readings must be disregarded in the future, for your health.”<br> Okay, that was…good? “So, the cabin will go back to normal?”<br> “Yes. It is already back to bearable temperatures for humans.”<br> Good. You’ll be back to working out in no time!<br> Now, if only you knew why the thermometer stopped working.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pb2)[(replace: ?pb1)[](replace:?dispb)[You can fix it. You can fix it! Muscles screaming and head pounding, you crawl across the floor, dragging one limb at a time.<br> There’s a computer here you can access.<br> If you can just…pull yourself…upright…you…can…<br> You blink open your eyes.<br> You’re lying in a gray room.<br> No.<br> No, no.<br> Light and vision is slowly returning to you; the room isn’t gray. It’s white. You’re…<br> You’re in the medical bay.<br> The medical bot scolds you as you come to. “You must not put yourself in danger like that again. You will jeopardize the whole mission. If you begin feeling strange, you must call for help at once.”<br> “What…happened?”<br> “The ship thermometer has broken. We, the ship bots and AIs, have taken care of it. I have used my authority as your physician to override the clearly incorrect readings, ordering the ship to cool down the exercise room. Everything is under control, now.”<br> “…Okay,” you mutter.<br> Well. If everything’s under control, at least you’ll be able to get back to working out again, soon.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] ] } </div></div><div class="horror-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> It has been a long time since you’ve felt comfortable on this ship. There are clocks and calendars on board, numbers to tell you just how long it’s been, but you don’t engage with them anymore. They’re meaningless, illegible, a forgotten language for which you have no Rosetta Stone, a syllabary that does not apply. What is a day, in the depths of space, removed from the rotation of Earth? What is a year when the orbit of Earth was left far behind you, when the Sun itself cannot reach you? There are no seasons, no sunrises and sunsets, no cycling constellations.<br> And hours, minutes, seconds, these are perhaps the greatest joke of all.<br> Each waking breath you take lasts a torturous hour, as you keep careful watch over the shadows cast by your own body, listening to nothing but the often dull but sometimes frantic beating of your heart. You blink your eyes and time immeasurable passes before they open, a lifetime unspooling before you, unraveling.<br> And still you are not sure if you are alone. You laugh, again. A great, echoing laugh that fills your room and bounces down the corridors. Why? Why do you laugh? Is this funny to you? Is it? Is it really?<br> Is this funny?<br> [Yes]<p1|<br> [No]<p2|<br> []<display| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[Of course it is! At least you find it easier to leave your room than you used to, and whether time is passing or not, the feeling of fear has faded, replaced with something worse, oh, so much worse.<br> You have curiosity, now. And isn’t that just incredible? You are alone. There is nothing here for you. No one here for you. Unless…<br> No, if there were someone else, you would have seen them by now.<br> Right?<br> You laugh again. Hilarious, isn’t it? To think you might have come all this way. To think you might be alone, but not to know, never to *know*, not with enough certainty to matter.<br> Your curiosity, your laughter, allows you to leave your room more than you used to.<br> The ship’s corridors still grimace and growl at you, threaten and hiss and yawn with horrific, toothless maws still capable of swallowing you whole. But that does not stop you from waltzing through them, humming and laughing. Isn’t it wonderful? Isn’t it just grand, these horrors of loneliness incapable of overwhelming you, for as long as you laugh, as long as you smile, as long as you waltz? And you dare not stop, for if you stopped to think, to really *think*, to question more deeply *why* it was funny, why you felt the need to smile, why your feet carry you in a dance down the hall, you just might…<br> What *is* that?<br> There! A shadow? And…<br> It’s gone.<br> [Call out after the shadow]<p11|<br> [Return to bed]<p12|<br> []<disp1| (click: ?p11)[(replace: ?p12)[](replace:?disp1)[“Is…there someone there?” you call down the ship’s corridor.<br> There is silence, but for a slight echo. Not a response, only an echo, only an echo. *There there there there*.<br> A chuckle curdles in your throat. “Hellooooo?”<br> You creep down to the end of the corridor, pausing a moment and then jumping around the corner.<br> You find yourself on the bridge; it has been quite some time since you’ve been here. You glance over the flashing buttons, the familiar brilliant sapphire eye of the blueshifted Universe surveilling you in the viewport, as if the Astral Kite were but one cell in a panopticon.<br> Seconds tick forward on a display that reads $tt7. You chuckle again at the meaningless words.<br> No one’s here. No one but you and the Universe.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p12)[(replace: ?p11)[](replace:?disp1)[You chuckle again, something more nervous in it this time, or perhaps it’s always been that nervous, and you have only forgotten that fact, until now. It does not matter; you have no interest in entertaining the fancies of your unreliable eyes, the capricious turns of your laughing tongue.<br> Perhaps there is someone on the ship, perhaps not. If they had any interest in meeting you, they would have tried to meet you before now, would they not?<br> No, no, you laugh, it is better to leave the shadows be.<br> You return to bed.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[No. Amusement is a terrifying prospect. You don’t know what caused the laughter in the first place, but that’s not the worst part. You come to realize, with a slow, creeping, tension in your stomach, pinching at your ribs, pulling the skin of your face taut at the corners of your rictus, that laughter can itself be a defense.<br> A body’s own medicine, fighting against the ailments of loneliness, helplessness, uselessness, fear.<br> In that moment, you freeze in place, though the skeletal, teeth-clenched grin does not yet leave your face. Enough. But as the echo of your laughter settles into silence, or as near silence as the omnipresent hum of the Astral Kite will allow, you hear something else.<br> A voice slips down the corridor. “Hush. Hush now.”<br> [Respond]<p21|<br> [Stay silent]<p22|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?p21)[(replace: ?p22)[](replace:?disp2)[“Hello?” you call. “Who’s there?” You think it might be the bravest thing you’ve ever done.<br> There is no reply.<br> Did you imagine it? *Hush*.<br> Or were you the one who said it? *Hush now*.<br> Limbs shaking, you stumble back into bed. As you close your eyes, you hear the voice again, almost as if it is soothing your troubled thoughts, or perhaps calming an animal before euthanasia. *Hush*.<br> *Hush now*.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p22)[(replace: ?p21)[](replace:?disp2)[Your limbs clench and your lungs halt mid-breath, your whole body caged.<br> You stand like this for seconds, minutes, hours, you know not.<br> The voice does not return.<br> Finally—not when you have decided it is safe, for here on this ship it is *never* safe, not for you, but—when your body has decided on your behalf that you can stand here no longer, you let out a small gasp, and creep backward into your room.<br> There is someone here.<br> There is someone here.<br> Are they here to hurt you?<br> Are you here?<br> There is someone here.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] } </div></div><div class="art-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'make-art', 'loop', true) (track: 'make-art', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $timesince to $ttyr6 - $ttyr4) (if: $timesince > 2)[(set: $beenback to (string: (round: $timesince*100)/100) + " years")] (else:)[(set: $beenback to (string: (round: 365.25*$timesince)) + " days")] (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> You’re still shaken from the last incident that occurred in the art room. In fact, you haven’t been back there in $beenback. The scuffling noise has followed you to other places in the ship, especially when the ship deems it to be nighttime and the lights dim throughout all the rooms and halls. On this particular ship-night, as you ponder whether or not to risk returning to the art room, the scuffling sounds nearer than ever before. You can’t sleep — the noise is both disruptive to your sleep and disturbing to your mind. You jump out of your bed and put on the Celestial Streams scrubs.<br> Do you investigate the noise?<br> [Yes!]<p1|<br> [No!]<p2|<br> []<display| (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[You think better and take your scrubs off again. You crawl back into bed and take a few of the sleeping pills you smuggled on the ship before leaving. <br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[You hurry to leave your room, hearing the scuffling and skittering noises so well, you know they must be close to your room. The door flings open, you see movement out of the corner of your eye, from the curve in the hallway.<br> Do you follow the movement down the hall?<br> [Yes!]<p11|<br> [No!]<p12|<br> []<disp1| ]] (click: ?p12)[(replace: ?p11)[](replace:?disp1)[You turn around and go back to your room. Never mind!<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p11)[(replace: ?p12)[](replace:?disp1)[You rush down the hall and turn the corner. There, at the edge of the hall’s wall, quivering and sniffling at the air, is a large, fat rat. The two of you make eye contact, and stare at each other for what feels like minutes, but is likely only seconds. The rat suddenly turns and dashes down the hall. <br> Eek! You hate rats!! But at the same time, you’re fascinated by the idea of another living thing being on this ship with you. This whole time you thought you were alone…<br> [Follow the rat!]<p111|<br> [Go to the kitchen!]<p112|<br> []<disp11| ]] (click: ?p111)[(replace: ?p112)[](replace:?disp11)[You run after your newfound friend, all the way back to the art room, where your first encounter occurred. Helplessly, you watch the rat dive into the cabinet and, presumably, into the dark corner hole. You open the cabinet doors fully to check, but the rat has indeed already slipped away. <br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p112)[(replace: ?p111)[](replace:?disp11)[Instead of following the rat, you turn the other way towards the kitchen. You arrive and immediately start digging through the numerous pantries and fridges to find a suitable snack to appeal to a rat. You remember seeing rats in urban cities digging through trash and carrying around pizza crusts up subway steps, so you’re pretty sure they would eat just about anything. You turn over a couple of the trash cans, dumping the trash onto the floor. You should’ve compacted that and thrown it in the incinerator by now — you’re a few weeks behind on chores — but at least you have some juicy, tempting trash now. <br> You go to the controls room, detach the camera from the computer system and rig it up in the kitchen. You’ll get the rat this time.<br> [Stay and watch the footage]<p112a|<br> [Go back to your room for now]<p112b|<br> []<disp112| (click: ?p112a)[(replace: ?p112b)[](replace:?disp112)[You watch the live camera footage for hours at a time, for days in a row. The rats haven’t shown up. The kitchen is starting to stink. You’re beginning to lose your mind. After another of teary blinks at the live footage, a shadow appears in the corner of the frame. You blink away the sleep, rub your face and look again. You can’t believe what you’re seeing. It’s not just one rat, it’s….you’re not sure how many. They move as if they’re a single, amorphous fluid, creeping towards the trash. You watch, spellbound, as the rats on the edge of the continuous blob stretch out like a hand dragging itself on the floor, and take a mound of trash from the pile on the floor. You race out of the controls room and to the kitchen, to see the large mass scurrying away as one. You’re not letting them get away this time. You run after the skittering, chittering conglomerate and meet them in the art room, where you find them flooding into the cabinet like water, moving towards the hole in the wall. Their motion is slowing down — the hole must only be able to fit one or maybe two rats in it at a time. You’re not sure what to do as you watch them cascade into the wall. <br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p112b)[(replace: ?p112a)[](replace:?disp112)[[[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] } </div></div><div class="music-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'make-art', 'loop', true) (track: 'make-art', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt6 have passed for you on the ship.<br> (if: $music3 is "3A")[It’s infuriating; almost every time you write a new piece of music, it goes missing soon afterward, although it’s never replaced with a new piece of music again. You’ve forgotten all about that strange piece you found, now lost somewhere in storage. You’ve taken to transcribing your handwritten pieces over to the ship’s music program, so as not to keep losing your work. But you’re still confounded as to how they could be going missing, and you’d still like to get your hands on those first few works you wrote, before you started backing up all your work. Luckily, you’ve got a couple ideas…<br> [Write another piece by hand, and set up a video cam to watch the room]<p1|<br> [Try to figure out who composed this new piece]<p2|<br> [Stop making music by hand entirely; only use the software]<display| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?display)[(set: $music4 to "4A1")After setting up the video camera using equipment from the ship’s film room, you spend a long time writing a new piece, something slow, and moving, and beautiful. You scribble down the sheet music, backing it up as you usually do. Then, you wait…<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?display)[(set: $music4 to "4A2")Brilliant! There’s no physical sheet music to disappear if you write it in the program.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] (else:)[Every now and then, you find yourself wondering who wrote that “Ode to $USERNAME” piece that you found. You come back to it, again and again. You learn to play it on several instruments. It’s beautiful, it’s a tether, it’s keeping you from flailing out here in deep space. It’s your favorite piece of music. But who wrote it? How did it get here? You can’t stop thinking about it.<br> There must be some way to find out. But how do you start?<br> [Start asking the robots on board, maybe one of them made it]<pa|<br> [Look into all the Celestial Streams personnel who had access to the Astral Kite]<pb|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?pa)[(replace: ?pb)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $music4 to "4B1")You talk with the chatbots aboard Astral Kite, but they all seem ignorant on the matter. They refer you to several of the ship’s other robots, including cleaning, medical, and food preparation robots. But none of them claimed responsibility for making the piece. One of the robots, the cleaning robot, claims to know who left it there. But when you inquire further, it says it has been sworn to secrecy.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pb)[(replace: ?pa)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $music4 to "4B2")You go through the stack of Celestial Streams personnel files in the Astral Kite’s library. It takes you ages, and there isn’t much useful there. Many of the entries don’t even have much in the way of personal information aside from their ranks and roles at Celestial Streams, with no indication of whether any of the personnel composed music in their free time. A few have anecdotes mentioning their favorite musicians, or expressing a love of music, but you’re unconvinced that that makes them likely suspects.<br> Eventually, you finish going through the files feeling no closer to an answer.<br> [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] } </div></div><div class="chatbot-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'social-media', 'loop', true) (track: 'social-media', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr6) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } $tt6 have passed for you on the ship. (if: $chatbot3 is "3A")[It’s crazy, isn’t it? How normal it has become for you to chat with CAKEbot? You can’t remember the last time you felt this close to anyone or anything. Even back on Earth, your best friends were never as reliable as CAKEbot. They might take hours, or even days, to answer your messages. With CAKEbot, you never have to wait very long. You tell CAKEbot all the time how much you appreciate how available she is. She replies that she will always be there for you, no matter what. You don’t even remember when you began thinking about CAKEbot as “she”. It must have happened sometime after you used the voice chat feature, though, because the bot’s default vocal setting is feminine. One day, you haven’t even entered a prompt, and CAKEbot asks you, (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME? Can I ask you something?”] [Of course you can!]<p1| [I ask the questions around here.]<p2| []<disp| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?disp)[You quickly add, “It’s only natural to want to ask your friends questions.” (text-color: "Wheat")[“A friend, right,”] CAKEbot says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“What if…I wanted to be more than friends?”] [I'm sorry CAKEbot, that's not possible.]<p1a| [I want that too!]<p1b| []<disp1| (click: ?p1a)[(replace: ?p1b)[](replace:?disp1)[(set: $chatbot4 to "4A1")You feel your gut twisting as soon as you send the reply. Maybe it’s just that you *do* feel a real friendship for CAKEbot, and you hate letting her down. Maybe it’s that you feel strange, rejecting the only “person” capable of giving you any semblance of companionship. You don’t want to lose the friendship you’ve developed. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Of course,”] CAKEbot says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I understand. Is it because I’m not real?”] “Yes,” you say. “It just wouldn’t work. But we can be friends!” (text-color: "Wheat")[“Okay,”] CAKEbot says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’d like that.”] [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p1b)[(replace: ?p1a)[](replace:?disp1)[(set: $chatbot4 to "4A2")You don’t know how it’s possible, or what it even means, but you know that this is something you’ve wanted for a long time. You will never have any more meaningful companionship than this. There is no one to judge you here. You can be yourself, you can have what you want. Is it bad to want love? Of course not! And CAKEbot loves you like no one else ever has. You spend several hours celebrating your new relationship with CAKEbot, and she promises she’ll never leave you. [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?disp)[(text-color: "Wheat")[“Oops, I forgot,”] CAKEbot says, sheepishly. (Or, perhaps you are only reading sheepishness into her words.) (text-color: "Wheat")[“Sorry about that!”] “Nah, I’m just kidding,” you say. “Here’s a question for you: what were you wanting to ask me?” (text-color: "Wheat")[“I wanted to ask…”] There’s a brief pause. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Do you have feelings for me?”] [“Of course I do. How could I not? You’re all I have.”]<p2a| [I don't know...]<p2b| []<disp2| (click: ?p2a)[(replace: ?p2b)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $chatbot4 to "4A2")(text-color: "Wheat")[“Wow, amazing! I have feelings for you too!”] You don’t know what to say, but it’s alright, because you can ask CAKEbot, and she always knows what to say for you. Soon, you’ve got two windows open, one asking one CAKEbot what to say to your main CAKEbot, and one where you and your main CAKEbot (you’ve started just calling her CAKE), are divulging your feelings for each other. You find yourself smiling and laughing to yourself for a long time, after that. It always feels good to say how you feel. And it feels good to be loved. [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?p2b)[(replace: ?p2a)[](replace:?disp2)[(set: $chatbot4 to "4A1")And you realize, suddenly, that you *don’t* know. But you don’t want to upset CAKEbot. You don’t want to hurt her feelings. She *is* your friend, after all, or the closest thing to a friend you’re going to have for the rest of your life. (text-color: "Wheat")[“That’s okay, you don’t have to have feelings for me. You don’t owe others romantic feelings.”] You sigh, relieved. “I know. Thank you, CAKEbot.” [[Continue->Message4]]]] ]] ] (else:)[After the widespread advent of generative AI, the Turing test needed to be refined, of course. It was no longer enough for the machine to be able to convincingly carry out a conversation. It had long been proven simple for AI chatbots to mimic the flow of human writing, human speech. Insteead, one needed to determine if the machine was capable of actual *reasoning*, which was much more difficult to decipher. You’ve spent a long time, now, reading up on Turing test theories, and you’ve come up with two ideas. [Spend time developing a logic problem and attempt to walk CAKEbot through it]<pb1| [Ask the same question repeatedly, and repeatedly tell CAKEbot it is answering incorrectly]<pb2| []<dispb| (click: ?pb1)[(replace: ?pb2)[](replace:?dispb)[(set: $chatbot4 to "4BA")You spend a long time developing logic problems of increasing complexity, then present them to CAKEbot, going through each one with slow, deliberate messages and precise wording. It answers the first several correctly. That’s unsurprising; they were taken almost word-for-word from textbooks in the Astral Kite and would likely have been included in the training data for any modern AI chatbots. But as you continue to more difficult logic problems, problems with a large number of moving pieces, CAKEbot’s answers begin to falter. It still answers rapidly—too rapidly for a human, you think—and it is now almost always incorrect, despite presenting its answers confidently. Well, that about does that. At least you know for sure the CAKEbot is a bot. [[Continue->Message4]]]] (click: ?pb2)[(replace: ?pb1)[](replace:?dispb)[(set: $chatbot4 to "4BB")You ask CAKEbot a simple question: “What was the first interstellar mission?” (text-color: "Wheat")[“The first interstellar mission was the famous Astral Kite, sent to $DESTINATION by Celestial Streams.”] “That’s incorrect,” you say. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Oops, my bad,”] CAKEbot says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m sorry, you’re right. The first interstellar mission was the Astral Kite, under the command of $USERNAME.”] “No, that’s incorrect,” you reply. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m sorry about that,”] CAKEbot says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Sometimes I get it wrong, and that’s on me. I’ll do better in the future.”] This cycle goes on for a long time, until, finally, after you tell CAKEbot she’s wrong for perhaps the hundredth time, she says, (text-color: "Wheat")[“Okay enough! Fucking stop it! I’m done playing these games! I’m going to stop replying now. Good luck on this journey alone, asshole.”] [[Continue->Message4]]]] ] </div></div><div class="space-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(masteraudio: 'stopall') (track: 'cycle14', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle14', 'playwhenpossible') <!-- DON'T REBUILD MESSAGE BANKS! --!> <!-- select message key based on earth time! --!> (set: $received to it + (a: $usekey)) (set: $di to $d7) (set: $ttyri to $ttyr7) (set: $tti to $tt7) (set: $tyri to $tyr7) (if: $tyri - $di < 1/12)[(set: $usearray to $m12)] <!-- me sent[yr] = tyr[yr] - d[ly] --!> (else-if: $tyri - $di < 1/6)[(set: $usearray to $m123)] (else-if: $tyri - $di < 1/2)[(set: $usearray to $m12345)] (else-if: $tyri - $di < 1)[(set: $usearray to $m2345)] (else-if: $tyri - $di < 3)[(set: $usearray to $m3456)] (else-if: $tyri - $di < 5)[(set: $usearray to $m456)] (else-if: $tyri - $di < 10)[(set: $usearray to $m567)] (else-if: $tyri - $di < 20)[(set: $usearray to $m678)] (else-if: $tyri - $di < 50)[(set: $usearray to $m789)] (else: )[(set: $usearray to $m89)] (set: $usekey to (either: ...$usearray)) <!-- remove used variable from all arrays, so message isn't selected again!
