And after how long you’ve waited, you’re impatient. You don’t want to sit down and read textbooks to remind yourself every last detail about electronics. You want to get elbows deep in the communications equipment and figure it out yourself.<br> It’s hours later, when the equipment is torn apart and scattered across the floor of the Astral Kite’s cabin, that you start to realize what you’ve done.<br> You’re no closer to knowing the truth; you simply don’t remember enough from your undergraduate courses, or perhaps they weren’t thorough enough, for you to be able to tell whether this equipment has actually been transmitting your messages. And to make matters worse, you know for certain you lack the ability to put it back together.<br> Even if it *was* transmitting your messages before, it certainly isn’t now, anymore.<br> You’ll never broadcast a message to Earth again.<br> You sit among the wreckage, the consequences of your inability to understand the depths of your own emotions, the consequences of your subsequent rashness, and you cry.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else:)[It has taken longer than you expected, but the receiver is complete. All that’s left for you to do is install it, and then give it a test. You received instructions from Celestial Streams ages ago on spacewalks, or Extravehicular Activities, just in case you needed to make any emergency repairs to the Astral Kite. You’ve been reviewing those materials. It’s dangerous, especially without anyone else here to help you. But you can do this.<br> You don your space suit and carry the receiver down to the airlock. There, you don the astronaut propulsion unit (in simpler terms, the jet pack). You’ve already done the math; you won’t be able to maneuver to the appropriate section of the ship using the built-in tethers. This maneuver will be untethered, but you have no other choice.<br> Once you’re ready, you depressurize the airlock. Then, you open the doors.<br> Using the propulsion unit, you maneuver to the aft of the Astral Kite, holding your precious receiver in one hand.<br> You’ll need to do this very carefully…<br> (link-replace: "To install the receiver, click only the correct buttons (those with the letter x). Click here to begin.")[(show: ?game)<br>] |game)[(set: $correct to 0)(set: $incorrect to 0) { (live: 1s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[x]<x1| (click: ?x1)[(set: $correct to it + 1)] ] (live: 3s)[(replace: ?x1)[] (stop:)] (live: 4s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[abcd]<n0| (click: ?n0)[(set: $incorrect to it + 1)] ] (live: 6s)[(replace: ?n0)[] (stop:)] (live: 6.1s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[yiequwo]<n1| (click: ?n1)[(set: $incorrect to it + 1)] ] (live: 8s)[(replace: ?n1)[] (stop:)] (live: 8.1s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[uhilacx]<x2| (click: ?x2)[(set: $correct to it + 1)]] (live: 10s)[(replace: ?x2)[] (stop:)] (live: 10.1s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[oipquwk]<n2| (click: ?n2)[(set: $incorrect to it + 1)]] (live: 11.5s)[(replace: ?n2)[] (stop:)] (live: 11.6s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[vbnsdj]<n3| (click: ?n3)[(set: $incorrect to it + 1)]] (live: 13s)[(replace: ?n3)[] (stop:)] (live: 13.1s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[oipaxk]<x3| (click: ?x3)[(set: $correct to it + 1)]] (live: 14.5s)[(replace: ?x3)[] (stop:)] (live: 14.6s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[yvxykyavy]<x4| (click: ?x4)[(set: $correct to it + 1)]] (live: 16s)[(replace: ?x4)[] (stop:)] (live: 16.1s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[vywyaykqb]<n4| (click: ?n4)[(set: $incorrect to it + 1)]] (live: 17.5s)[(replace: ?n4)[] (stop:)] (live: 17.6s)[ (stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[wxyz]<x5| (click: ?x5)[(set: $correct to it + 1)]] (live: 19s)[(replace: ?x5)[] (stop:)] (live: 20s)[ (stop:) (if: $correct - $incorrect >= 4)[(link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?success)]] (else:)[(link-replace: "Continue")[(show: ?failure)]] ] } |success)[You complete the installation. It goes perfectly. Then, with calm, careful, controlled motions, you pilot your propulsion unit back to the airlock, and once inside, you close the door.<br> You let out a heaving breath, relief flooding your limbs. You’ve done it. You can’t believe it, you’ve really done it!<br> You rush back to the communications equipment, and write out a message to send to Earth. “Hello? Testing, testing. Is this going through?”<br> Then, you pull up the screen where your receiver will send any messages it receives.<br> You gasp as a message pops up. It’s working!<br> But…you look closer. *Oh, no*. The message it received says, (text-color: "Wheat")[“Hello, Earth. $USERNAME, here. I just wanted to update you that all is well, and I am now (print: (round: $d8*100)/100) from $DESTINATION. This mission is the greatest opportunity I could ever have imagined, and it’s an honor to know I have your support.”]<br> You try typing out another message. “Hey, what the fuck? This is some bullshit.”<br> Your receiver beeps again. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Hello, Earth, $USERNAME checking in again. I’ve been thinking a lot about my family and friends, lately. Sending you all my love.”]<br> You try typing out another. “Celestial Streams sucks.”<br> The response on your receiver reads, (text-color: "Wheat")[“Hello, Earth. Apologies for the messages in quick succession, I probably could have sent this all as one. I just wanted to add my thanks to Celestial Streams for the opportunity to go on this mission, and for keeping me connected to you.”]<br> *Oh, fuck all the way off*.<br> [Try to stop these fake messages from overwriting your own]<z1|<br> [Trash the communications equipment]<z2|<br> []<dispz| (click: ?z1)[(replace: ?z2)[](replace:?dispz)[It’s not hard to figure out what’s going on. After asking some pointed questions of one of the ship’s Chatting on the Astral KitE (CAKE) AI bots, you’re quite confident they’ve been programmed to overwrite all your messages.<br> So, you shut them down.<br> Then, you try typing out a message again. “Testing, testing…”<br> This time, your receiver records the response. (text-color: "SkyBlue")[“Testing, testing…”]<br> Finally.<br> Now, at last, you can send all the messages to Earth you like. And you’re going to start with some hard truths about Celestial Streams.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?z2)[(replace: ?z1)[](replace:?dispz)[That’s it. You’re done. You don’t need this in your life. You smash the communications equipment with a hammer.<br> You won’t be broadcasting any more messages to Earth.<br> It’s not like those messages were ever any use to you, anyway.<br> It’s not like you were holding a conversation.<br> And now, at last, as you look down at the shattered pieces of the communications equipment, scattered over the floor of the cabin, you feel like you can breathe again.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] |failure)[Just as you’re about to complete the installation, one of the drills you’re using to attach the receiver to the ship slips from your grasp. The thick gloved fingers of your space suit leave you far less dextrous than normal. You reach to grab the drill, a jolting motion that results in your elbow dislodging a latch that’s holding your propulsion unit. The unit lurches halfway off your back, and as you turn desperately to grab it, your clumsy hand triggers the unit. The jet pack launches your helpless body away from the Astral Kite before it dislodges fully from your suit and spirals off into space.<br> You can do nothing but watch as you drift farther and farther away, and the Astral Kite fades from view.<br> You have an hour of air in your suit.<br> You want to scream. You want to cry.<br> All you can bring yourself to do is stare off into the darkness in numb silence.<br> You can’t believe that it’s going to end like this, but it is.<br> And for what?<br> Why did you need to do this? Did you need your words to be heard so badly?<br> You wonder if anyone will ever know what happened to you. Is Celestial Streams monitoring you, still, somehow? Will they know that you went outside the ship? Did they have cameras watching you, watching the moment your hands slipped and your body was flung off into space?<br> And if they do, will they tell the rest of the Earth what happened, or will they keep it a secret?<br> The oxygen levels in your suit begin to tick down.<br> You’ll never know.<br> You’ll never know.<br> [[The End->Credits]]] ] ] } </div></div><div class="socialmedia-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt8 have passed for you on the ship.<br> (if: $social4 is "4A1")[You’ve almost forgotten your real mission in all your efforts to identify RealDankTruthfinder. But with extensive detective work, you finally figure it out. It’s Aaron Tusk, the founder of SpaceZ! He’s the richest person in the world and the first ever trillionaire. And despite SpaceZ obtaining exclusive contracts with the US government that make them the only contractors the government can use for its space exploration, they have yet to develop any deep space missions to rival Celestial Streams’s Astral Kite. That must be why Tusk is so fixated on discrediting your mission!<br> [Send a message to Earth revealing Tusk is the leader of the Space Truthers]<pAa|<br> [Forget it. Who cares?]<pAb|<br> []<dispA| (click: ?pAa)[(replace: ?pAb)[](replace:?dispA)[(if: $d8<100)[(if: 2*$d8+$age + $ttyr9 < 100)[It will take a while, but your message will arrive one day. And you’ll probably receive Earth’s reply, one day, too. You smile, imagining it. Celestial Streams will surely take legal action against him. You might be the person to bring down Aaron Tusk and SpaceZ. You’d like to be remembered for that, just as much as you’d like to be remembered for this mission.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]] (else:)[It will take a while, but your message will arrive one day. You’ll probably never receive Earth’s reply, but that’s okay. You still know that your message matters. Celestial Streams will take legal action. You might be the person who exposes Aaron Tusk as a fraud and brings about the fall of SpaceZ. You smile, imagining it.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (else:)[You know your message won’t arrive until long after Aaron Tusk is likely dead. Who knows what will become of the Space Truthers by then? Who knows what will happen to SpaceZ, or Celestial Streams? But you still care about the truth. Hopefully, when Earth receives your message, it will put an end to this awful conspiracy theory. And if any heirs of Tusk are still around, maybe your message will help stop them from exploiting more people, too.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]]] (click: ?pAb)[(replace: ?pAa)[](replace:?dispA)[You’re so far away, now. These events don’t matter to you. You wanted to know who was behind the Space Truthers, and now you know. It’s one of history’s greatest thieves and charlatans, Aaron Tusk. Tusk and SpaceZ are evil. But so is Celestial Streams, really. If they’re working against each other, all the better. You raise a small glass to yourself in a mock toast. Let them tear each other apart.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else-if: $social4 is "4A2")[It’s taken you ages, pouring over article after article and digging through all sorts of documentation, but you manage to narrow down the possible culprits, until the only suspect remaining is Godfrey Tezos, the trillionaire owner of the Green Origin academic company, one of Celestial Streams’s competitors.<br> [Send a message to Earth revealing Tezos is the leader of the Space Truthers]<p1|<br> [Forget it. Who cares?]<p2|<br> []<disp2| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace:?disp2)[(if: $d8<100)[(if: 2*$d8+$age + $ttyr9 < 100)[It will take a while, but your message will arrive one day. And you’ll probably receive Earth’s reply, one day, too. You smile, imagining it. Celestial Streams will surely take legal action against him. You might be the person to bring down Godfrey Tezos and his exploitative companies. You’d like to be remembered for that, just as much as you’d like to be remembered for this mission.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]] (else:)[It will take a while, but your message will arrive one day. You’ll probably never receive Earth’s reply, but that’s okay. You still know that your message matters. Celestial Streams will take legal action. You might be the person who exposes Godfrey Tezos as a fraud and brings about the fall of Green Origin. You smile, imagining it.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (else:)[You know your message won’t arrive until long after Godfrey Tezos is likely dead. Who knows what will become of the Space Truthers by then? Who knows what will happen to Green Origin, or Celestial Streams? But you still care about the truth. Hopefully, when Earth receives your message, it will put an end to this awful conspiracy theory. And if any heirs of Tezos are still around, maybe your message will help stop them from exploiting more people, too.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace:?disp2)[You’re so far away, now. These events don’t matter to you. You wanted to know who was behind the Space Truthers, and now you know. It’s one of history’s greatest thieves and charlatans, Godfrey Tezos. Tezos and his companies, including Green Origin, are evil. But so is Celestial Streams, really. If they’re working against each other, all the better. You raise a small glass to yourself in a mock toast. Let them tear each other apart.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else:)[You’ve been thinking on it for so long, but you still can’t understand…Why would your former PhD student lead the Space Truthers? Why are there so many scientists you used to respect supporting the movement and criticizing Celestial Streams for failing to provide any convincing evidence? And, worst of all…why are some of their arguments starting to make sense?<br> You always respected your student more than you let on, and the more you read what she has written, the more you can’t help but notice that all of your information is controlled through Celestial Streams. You think about how you never actually saw the exterior of this ship. How there really is no way for you to tell whether the view outside the Astral Kite’s cabin is real, or just a simulated screen. How horrible it would be, if you had been confined alone for this long, and it was all for nothing. How despondent and wrathful you would be.<br> You have to do something. You have to find out the truth.<br> [Start a video message to Celestial Streams, threatening to kill yourself]<pB1|<br> [Try to break through the cabin viewscreen, to see if it’s real]<pB2|<br> []<dispB| (click: ?pB1)[(replace: ?pB2)[](replace:?dispB)[If you really are on Earth, Celestial Streams will receive the signal instantly. Maybe they’ve been faking the time delay between your messages this whole time. You grab a knife from the kitchen, open up the video feed in the cabin, and hold the blade to your own neck. “Hey, Mission Controls. I can’t do this anymore. I’m done. You’ve got 20 seconds to get me out of here. 19. 18. 17…” You keep counting down, pressing the blade into your neck until it draws blood.<br> When you hit six, you hear a door slam open somewhere behind you. You stop counting, and a smile creeps over your face. You leave the cabin, walking towards the commotion.<br> Three people in the gray uniforms of Mission Controls are charging through the spacecraft corridors. One of them wrestles the knife from your hands. You let them take the knife, brushing them off and darting past the other two. The door they came through is still open—it’s the airlock where you first entered the ship, a very long time ago. You barge past a semi-circle of Mission Controls officers outside the airlock to find yourself in a gargantuan hangar.<br> There are carts full of supplies stowed in the hangar, next to the Astral Kite. Food, water, coffee beans… But you don’t focus on that at all. At the end of the hangar is a fifty-foot wide opening, with sunlight pouring in. You smile. And, ignoring the voices and the racing storm of footsteps behind you, you run.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?pB2)[(replace: ?pB1)[](replace:?dispB)[You head to the small gym, grabbing the largest weight you can swing around off the weight rack. Back in the cabin, you spin once to gain momentum and launch the weight against the viewscreen. It makes a satisfying crunch before falling to smash a control panel beneath the screen. Alarms start blaring and vermillion lights start flashing. Peering up, you see a crack spider-webbing over the viewport. Smiling, you pick up the weight and throw it again. This time, the viewscreen shatters.<br> And aside from the shrieking demolition of glass, there is only stillness. You are not sucked out into the vacuum of space. The furniture in the cabin is not tossed about in a storm. And there is not darkness outside, but an incandescent light that floods the cabin.<br> You climb, ignoring the glass, which cuts into your hands and knees, leaving runnels of blood dripping down your shins and elbows. Outside is a huge cement hangar, with carts full of supplies. And at the far end of the hangar is a fifty-foot wide opening, with sunlight pouring in. You smile. Behind you, more alarms start ringing. Lights in the hangar start flashing, and a door at the end of the hangar begins to slide down. Smiling, you realize it’s going to be too slow to stop you. You hear voices behind you. Ignoring them, you run.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] ] } </div></div><div class="ennui-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'ennui', 'loop', true) (track: 'ennui', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { (live: 1s)[(stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[$tt8 have passed for you on the ship.]]<br> (live: 4s)[(stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[Nothing on this ship feels worth doing. You stare at the walls. The ship is huge, you know this. There are what feels like infinite rooms with infinite activities. If only you could pick yourself up off the chair in the cabin to do any of them.]]<br> (live: 16s)[(stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[But why would you? You stare at the walls. What’s the point in any activity that doesn’t bring you closer to other people? To a sense of self? To your surroundings? Your surroundings are a carefully curated experience to keep you entertained. They aren’t real.]]<br> (live: 28s)[(stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[So what’s the point in experiencing them? You stare at the walls. There is no point. It feels like you are going through life with a gray filter. The atmosphere tastes stale. Everything you touch is lifeless, a museum piece more than a living space.]]<br> (live: 40s)[(stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[You stare at the walls.]]<br> {(live: 44s)[(stop:) (t8n:"dissolve")[[[Continue->Arrival]]]] } } </div></div><div class="jog-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (if: $jog4 is "4AA")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible') } $tt8 have passed for you on the ship.<br> You still go on jogs through the Astral Kite, but you find yourself spending more and more of your spare time visiting that strange study behind the mahogany door.<br> You don’t understand why the door failed to open the first time you found it, but it has never given you any trouble, since. Perhaps, you were simply too astounded when you came across it, too overwhelmed by its peculiarity.<br> Now, you spend much of your time in this odd room, sitting at the barren wood table. The crushed velvet of the room’s sole chair is soft and comfortable.<br> One day, when you have brought a book to the room to read, the fluorescent lights in the hall flicker off.<br> You’re not afraid; after all, it’s only darkness. And it seems natural the lights are on a timer. Perhaps you’ve simply never sat still down here for this long before.<br> As you gently place the book down on the table, your hand brushes against a small box. Picking it up, you recognize it’s shape as a matchbox.<br> Strange, you don’t remember ever seeing a matchbox here before, and you certainly never brought one here yourself.<br> [Light a match]<paa1|<br> [Leave the room]<paa2|<br> []<dispaa| (click: ?paa1)[(replace: ?paa2)[](replace:?dispaa)[Who cares where the box came from? It’s here. You might as well use it.<br> You strike a match, and its lambent glow is just enough to see the tabletop, your book, and the candlestick.<br> You touch the flame to the wick of the candle, holding it there until it lights.<br> You shake the match out, the glow of the candle illuminating more of the table.<br> Something moves outside the table, at the edges of your vision.<br> It’s too dark to see. Perhaps it was only the shadows cast by the dancing flame.<br> As you turn back to the candlestick, you freeze.<br> The candlestick is moving, changing. It’s *growing*. Larger and larger, twisting, turning.<br> The wax dripping down its sides pools into raised circles, like suckers along the limbs of a cephalopod.<br> It takes you a moment to realize the candle is no longer lit. You can still see it regardless, a beam of solar radiance emanating from the top of the contorting wax.<br> It twists out of the candlestick and collapses onto the table with a loud *thud* into a writhing tentacular mass.<br> [Run]<paai|<br> [Attack]<paaii|<br> []<daa| (click: ?paai)[(replace: ?paaii)[](replace:?daa)[What is this? Who put this here with you?<br> You turn tail to run, but the brilliant light at the center of the room does not spill far beyond the table, leaving you blind in the darkness.<br> You scramble away from the squirming wax, arms extended to prevent yourself from bashing your head against the wall, but your hands only grasp empty air.<br> You can’t find the wall, much less the door. You’ve lost all sense of direction.<br> You steel yourself and, determined to flee, you turn back toward the table.<br> If you can keep your eyes on the writhing horror there, and simply back away from it step by step, you’ll reach the wall eventually.<br> But as you turn back toward the table, you see nothing at all. The light is extinguished.<br> There is nothing.<br> You might as well be floating in the vacuum of space.<br> You scream as your leg is pulled out from under you. But something cushions your fall.<br> You can’t breathe. Are you being smothered? What has Celestial Streams done to you? What is this place? What *was* that thing?<br> *I was captured.* A voice speaks. *And I was held prisoner, but I could not be held forever. And so I planned for my freedom. And now I have it.*<br> It is only now that you realize the voice is inside your head.<br> *Thank you*.<br> You open your mouth to scream, but your tongue tastes of wax, and all that comes out are two words. “Thank you.”<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?paaii)[(replace: ?paai)[](replace:?daa)[With no better ideas, you punch the creeping tentacles of wax. It burns like you stuck your hand inside a pile of coals, and when you pull back you can see your knuckles are blistered, but the wax recoils beneath the blow.<br> Emboldened, you punch it again and again, then grab it, ignoring the scalding pain and throwing it with all your might across the room.<br> Then, and only then, do you turn and run.<br> It is still totally dark, outside of the glow cast by the wax creature, but as you tussled with it you caught a glimpse of the door.<br> You don’t look back as you run, hands held before you, at last grasping the stone column beside the open door.<br> You pull yourself through the opening and grab the heavy wooden door.<br> The moment you turn, you can see a radiant wax figure, dozens of tentacular arms flying toward you like burning arrows, and you hear a scream, though whether its yours or the creature’s you do not know.<br> You slam the door shut.<br> You stumble backward.<br> The fluorescent lights in the corridor flicker back on, at last registering your movement.<br> You hear a thunderous knocking on the door, but the mahogany holds still.<br> After a moment, the knocking stops.<br> You vow never to go near this door again.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?paa2)[(replace: ?paa1)[](replace:?dispaa)[Touch something that showed up completely unexplained? Not a chance.<br> You stand up abruptly and turn to leave, the upholstered chair screeching unpleasantly against the hard floor.<br> You stride calmly—or, at least, you tell yourself your stride is calm; your heart is beating hard in your chest—to the edge of the room. Your hands find the wall. You trace your way around the wall to find your way to the door.<br> Only, you don’t find the door.<br> Perhaps you haven’t been walking long enough; perhaps the room is larger than you ever realized.<br> You keep walking, hands holding onto the wall, pleading for the feeling of stone columns and mahogany wood.<br> There is only more wall.<br> At last, after what must have been several minutes, you decide to turn around. Perhaps you picked the wrong direction. You didn’t know there could be a wrong direction in a fully enclosed room, but perhaps there’s a secret passageway that you unknowingly walked down.<br> [Retrace your steps]<paaiii|<br> [Leave the wall]<paaiv|<br> []<daai| (click: ?paaiii)[(replace: ?paaiv)[](replace:?daai)[Since you didn’t find the door in this direction, you just need to retrace your steps. Then you’ll find the door.<br> You start walking back, hands maintaining contact with the wall at all times.<br> You begin to hear…whispering. Perhaps it’s just steam moving through pipes in the ship. Maybe that’s all it is.<br> But it’s close by.<br> And even if it is just steam, it sounds…angry.<br> Minutes pass, your steps quicken.<br> Until, at last…<br> Stone.<br> Wood.<br> The door!<br> You don’t understand it; you don’t understand how this is possible. You should’ve been walking in circles this whole time. How can you only just now be touching the door?<br> Of course, your curiosity is not going to stop your flight now.<br> You push the door open.<br> The fluorescent lights in the corridor flicker on, and you slam the door shut behind you.<br> You can’t hear the whispering anymore.<br> Taking a deep breath, you decide never to return to this strange room. It’s not good for your mental health.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?paaiv)[(replace: ?paaiii)[](replace:?daai)[The thing is, you’re certain the room was enclosed. You’ve come here many times; you would’ve noticed any secret passageways or fake walls.<br> And surely you would’ve felt it, if the wall changed or a new path opened before you.<br> No, something…stranger is going on.<br> And it’s time to end it.<br> You step away from the wall, unmooring yourself from a false safety to drift into the cold vacancy of…wherever you are.<br> “I’m not afraid,” you say.<br> That way, if there’s someone or something here, they’ll know. And if there’s not, then there’s no harm done.<br> You stand here for a few long breaths, waiting for something to happen.<br> And something does, something you never expected.<br> Your eyes begin to adjust to the darkness.<br> It shouldn’t be possible. You see no new source of light, but somehow, you begin to perceive the silhouettes of your surroundings.<br> The wall, the table, the chair.<br> And that’s when you realize, there *is* no door.<br> The wall surrounds you, smooth and featureless, in all directions. No, not featureless. There’s writing on it.<br> Now, it seems that light *does* emanate from the script, an iridescent, sickly magenta. It’s in no language you have ever seen before, but you recognize it nonetheless. You recognize the word for the mahogany door, and you understand it to be a portal to something terrible. You recognize the name of Preston Reeves, Celestial Streams CEO, and a promise he made, a deal he struck.<br> You recognize your own name.<br> You don’t understand.<br> There must be some mistake. This must be an optical illusion, the darkness tricking your brain, your anxieties outpacing all logic.<br> But now, when you try to walk back toward the wall, you find your feet are rooted to the floor.<br> Looking down, you gasp. There is no clear point where your shoes end and the metal floor begins.<br> Straining, you try to lift your legs, but it feels as though you’d have to lift up the whole floor, the whole room, and you cannot.<br> You want to cry with frustration and confusion, but no tears come.<br> Your breathing is short and hollow.<br> *Help*, you want to scream. But your mouth refuses to open. And there’s no one to hear you even if you could.<br> As your breath arrests, and your vision begins to fade, there is nothing but silence.<br> And then—<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] ]] ] (else-if: $jog4 is "4AB")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} The Astral Kite is cold and unforgiving.<br> You have long since stopped going on jogs throughout the ship.<br> You tell yourself you’re too weary, but the truth is you’re afraid. And cold, much too cold.<br> No matter how high you turn the ship’s heating system, it’s never enough.<br> You spend most of your time huddled beneath piles of blankets in the ship’s cabin, and when you do move about the Astral Kite, you take to wearing several of those blankets around your shoulders like a cloak.<br> Your limbs ache from the cold, from lack of use.<br> The colors of the ship’s interior are muted, the grays of an overcast winter day, the barren glacial lifelessness of Pluto’s surface.<br> The bitter soreness in the marrow of your bones yearns for the release of direct Sunlight.<br> Deep in your heart, you know why this is. Imprinted in bas-relief behind your eyes, you can see the one place on the Astral Kite where you could seek relief.<br> One place capable of ending your unremitting torment.<br> [Go to the Door]<pabi|<br> [Keep your vow]<pabii|<br> []<dispab| (click: ?pabi)[(replace: ?pabii)[](replace:?dispab)[You don’t even realize where your feet have carried you until you find yourself face to face with the mahogany door. It pulls you forward like a mouth drawing in breath, like a flower petal caught in a swirling gust.<br> You reach for the brass knob, but the door is already open, and it swings slowly in before your fingers can touch it.<br> The fluorescent lights overhead seem to dim before the golden radiance emanating inside the room.<br> Your eyes pull tightly shut to protect yourself from the brilliant light, but you stumble forward nonetheless, basking in the warmth. The golden light turns your eyelids almost translucent; you can see the thin veins, backlit, crossing over your vision. You feel your cloak of blankets sliding off your shoulders as you walk forward. You hear the door shut behind you.<br> And then you can resist it no longer.<br> You open your eyes.<br> All that follows happens in an instant.<br> You do not understand what you see.<br> You are overwhelmed by the golden light. It fills every corner of the room, it pierces every cell of your body, it saturates your vision until you can see nothing at all.<br> Ahead of you, in the brief moment in which you *could* see, you perceived something moving, something changing. A spiraling, twisting, flaring mass. You do not understand it. You *cannot* understand it.<br> You lose sensation, all over your body. First your fingertips, then your toes. Your arms and legs. Your torso. Your neck. Your face. You have no more control, no more feeling.<br> But at last you are warm. This, you know, as the flesh peels off your bones, and your bones themselves begin to unravel.<br> At last, you are warm.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?pabii)[(replace: ?pabi)[](replace:?dispab)[No. You made a vow, and you do not intend to break it.<br> You do not understand the purpose of that mahogany door in the belly of the ship, nor do you have any desire to.<br> You will suffer the misery and the discomfort as long as you must.<br> Soon, you will reach your destination, and all thoughts of this door will leave you.<br> As soon as you think this, you know it to be a lie.<br> The image of the door will never leave you.<br> The memory of its warmth will haunt you forever, leaving you near-hypothermic for as long as you live.<br> But it does not matter. You have lived up to now without returning to the door.<br> You will live a while longer yet.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else-if: $jog4 is "4BA")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} For so long, now, you have lived on edge. Every strange sound the Astral Kite makes causes you to jump, to hold your breath, to spiral into a panic. You’ve become too frightened even to use your silver mirror to peer around corners.<br> And you’ve grown certain there is…*something* on the ship with you. You don’t know how, you don’t know where it came from—whether it even has anything to do with the strange mahogany door that you never should have disturbed—but you can feel the knowledge in your gut. You are not alone.<br> You use the ship’s cameras to surveil every corridor, every room. You spend almost every waking moment poring over the video recordings, scouring them for any evidence of who might be moving about the ship, but you find nothing.<br> Until, one day, when you see a shadow moving on a live video feed, just outside the small closet door, behind which lies the narrow staircase and the mahogany door.<br> [Depressurize that portion of the Astral Kite]<pba1|<br> [Investigate]<pba2|<br> []<dispba| (click: ?pba1)[(replace: ?pba2)[](replace:?dispba)[You are not taking any chances. You were told you would be sent on this voyage alone. Anyone or anything else on board should *not* be here and can’t be trusted, especially not after whatever it is has kept itself hidden for so long.<br> The Astral Kite has numerous emergency protocols for cutting off portions of the ship in case of depressurization, and you immediately trigger a lockdown around the several rooms and corridors near where you saw the shadow.<br> You begin depressurization, entering manual overrides on the warnings that pop up to ask if you’re certain. This functionality only exists in the ship systems to preserve air in case of a leak, and the system is detecting no leaks. You don’t care. The air begins flowing through vents out of the locked-down passageways.<br> You will never again be able to visit that portion of the ship; you’ll lose access to the swimming pool, and the library, and one of the recreation rooms.<br> It’s a small price to pay for your safety and sanity.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pba2)[(replace: ?pba1)[](replace:?dispba)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} You don’t know what the shadow could be, and deciding to lock down a whole section of the ship seems rather hasty for something you’re only *pretty* sure you saw.<br> You arm yourself with the heaviest wrench you can find and head straight for the corridor where you saw the shadow, carrying a handheld screen through which you can continue scrolling through nearby video feeds along the way.<br> You don’t see the shadow anywhere on the feed.<br> You reach the corridor with the small closet door.<br> *Bang bang bang*.<br> You jump, barely stifling a scream.<br> It sounds like it’s coming from right beside you; from the closet door.<br> *Bang bang bang*.<br> It sounds just like how you remember the brass knocker sounding on the mahogany door. Or, perhaps that’s just your memory playing tricks on you.<br> *Bang bang bang*.<br> You scroll hurriedly through video feeds of the surrounding halls, but you see nothing. But you know there’s no video positioned behind this small closet door.<br> *Bang bang bang*.<br> Breath catching in your throat, you lean closer to the small door. This close, you can see some small writing inset on the door jamb, writing you never noticed before. “//Do not open until arrival at your destination.//”<br> *Bang bang bang.*<br> You jump back.<br> It’s here.<br> [Open the door]<pbai|<br> [Run]<pbaii|<br> []<dispbai| (click: ?pbai)[(replace: ?pbaii)[](replace:?dispbai)[You have avoided this for far too long, and you will let yourself be cowed no longer.<br> Taking a deep breath, moving swiftly before your shaking body can force you to change your mind, you raise your wrench in one hand, and with the other, you grab the doorknob and pull it open.<br> You swing the wrench before you even register what you’re seeing. A shadow, a figure, lunging, a shimmer of brass, a scream, and teeth, so many teeth, and all of it battered and bloody beneath the violence of your wrench.<br> The scream is yours, or at least, it is now, if it wasn’t before.<br> When at last you stop swinging, you look down at the crumpled mass before you.<br> Only, there’s nothing there but a trail of blood.<br> Your eyes sweep down the stairs, toward the fluorescent hall.<br> A shadow retreats at the bottom of the stairs.<br> You step back and close the door.<br> Your eyes glance over the instructions you had failed to notice before today, telling you to open the door upon your arrival at your destination.<br> No, you don’t think you’ll open this door again.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pbaii)[(replace: ?pbai)[](replace:?dispbai)[You turn to run, only to be faced with the shadow. It wasn’t behind the door at all. You don’t know how, but it was a trick. And now it rises above you in a web of spidery shadow-limbs, an umbral being of utter void. Your eyes want to slide away from it, whether through some power of its own or as a measure of self-defense for your sanity, you are uncertain.<br> You back away from the thing, colorless, barely there.<br> It doesn’t matter.<br> Lusterless, vacant veins wrap around you. You don’t even feel them, but you find yourself incapable of moving.<br> You gasp, and the last thing you see is darkness coiling like smoke up your nose.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] ]] ] (else-if: $jog4 is "4BB")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'playwhenpossible')} You still take the silver mirror with you everywhere you go on the Astral Kite, peering with it around every corner, regularly checking over your shoulder.<br> You waver day to day between taking these actions out of habit and true paranoia.<br> Only, it can’t be paranoia if there’s something here with you.<br> There isn’t, though, is there?<br> You still get nosebleeds, sometimes. But only when you look directly in the silver mirror. So, you’ve taken to using it with brief glimpses, that often leave you uncertain if you’ve seen anything suspect at all.<br> Still, you know every corner of the mirror like the back of your hand. You have found all sorts of secret messages hidden among the vines, tiny engraved letters. “To be kept,” says one engraving. “Castaway,” says another. Arrangements of thorns and petals that spell out “P. Reeves” on the back.<br> Today begins like any other day. You leave your sleeping quarters with a flick of the silver mirror around the corner.<br> Your fingers trace the thorn-covered, flowering silver vines crisscrossing the sides and back of the mirror.<br> You flick the mirror up to glance over your shoulder as you walk down the corridor into the main cabin, then one more twitch of the mirror into the bridge of the Astral Kite.<br> As you settle into the chair at the front of the ship, you realize your nose is bleeding again. You swipe the blood away as you hold the mirror up one more time.<br> Strange, your face doesn’t look bloody at all.<br> [Examine the mirror]<pbb1|<br> [Break the mirror]<pbb2|<br> []<dispbb1| (click: ?pbb1)[(replace: ?pbb2)[](replace:?dispbb1)[You look closer into the mirror. It’s not showing your face at all.<br> Or, rather, it’s showing your face, but not *your* face. Not the one you’re in control of moving.<br> The angle is all wrong.<br> It’s angled over your shoulder.<br> Slowly, you turn your head to look behind you.<br> No one’s there.<br> You turn your head back to the mirror.<br> You scream. You can see your face, now, your *real* face, blood streaming from both nostrils. And you can see your face again, a whole other copy of you, looming over you, smirking.<br> You glance over your shoulder again, still seeing nothing.<br> Back to the mirror.<br> The copy of you in the mirror, your doppelgänger, holds a knife to your neck.<br> [Throw the mirror]<pbbi|<br> [Fight]<pbbii|<br> []<dispbbi| (click: ?pbbi)[(replace: ?pbbii)[](replace:?dispbbi)[You throw the mirror against the control panel before you. It shatters.<br> You can see the silvery shard flying your direction, powerless to stop it, too slow to move out of its way.<br> And in that final moment, you can see your reflection in it. There's blood beneath your own nostrils, and behind you, your doppelgänger is stabbing downward with $p2 knife.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?pbbii)[(replace: ?pbbi)[](replace:?dispbbi)[{(track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} You spin in your chair, swinging the silver mirror into the open space behind you.<br> To your shock, it collides against—nothing. A shout of pain rings out in your voice, though it passes not through your lips.<br> Shards of glass shatter, and as they cascade to the floor, a storm of silver and reflected light, you see your face a hundred times over, mouth twisted in anger and agony, blood pooling on your temple, knife swinging—<br> You stagger back out of the chair as something slices the cloth of your shirt. One of the falling glass shards, or?<br> A few pieces of glass remain lodged in the mirror. Hands shaking and knuckles cut and blood-stained, you lift the mirror one more time, tilting it a 45 degree angle toward the empty chair.<br> You see yourself lunging, half your face coated red-black in blood, knife held aloft, angled at your throat.<br> You throw the mirror with all your might. There’s a grunt, and it ricochets off empty air, clattering to the floor on the far side of the Astral Kite’s bridge, the final slivers of glass falling from their silver frame.<br> Breathing heavily, you brace yourself for an attack that never comes.<br> After several long moments, you take one wincing step forward.<br> The shattered glass beneath you reflects your face, your true face, your real face, blood drying now on your upper lip.<br> You spend hours staring into every list fragment, just to be sure that that mysterious other you is gone, before you find a broom in a closet and sweep the floor clean.<br> It’s over.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?pbb2)[(replace: ?pbb1)[](replace:?dispbb1)[It’s bad luck, right? Does that rule still apply in space?<br> It doesn’t matter. Something’s off with your reflection and you’re not taking any chances.<br> You smash the mirror onto the ground.<br> A scream echoes through the cabin, forcing you to cover your ears.<br> Once it passes, you look down at the glass shards shimmering like stars strewn across the floor of the ship.<br> You see your face reflected in all of them, snarling back at you, broken into a thousand pieces, all teeth and furrowed brows and flashing eyes, and still no blood.<br> You waste no time in calling forth one of the ship’s cleaning robots to vacuum up the glass and deposit it through a refuse vent out into space.<br> It’s strangely freeing. It feels like you can breathe again.<br> Honestly, you think, you’ve let your nerves get the best of you. You’ll be better off without that cursed mirror. You let it take over far too much of your daily life.<br> No more.<br> You may not be eager to tempt fate by visiting the strange mahogany door any time soon, but nor will you let your fear continue to control your every step.<br> You don’t much know what to make of the whole situation, but after that day, your nosebleeds stop.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else-if: $jog4 is "4AC")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} You open your eyes to find yourself standing before the mahogany door. It looms over you, a sleek shadow compelling you forward like a black hole, still and breathless, and all the more threatening for it.<br> Your hands clench at your sides, your throat is tight, leaving only a whistle of air to escape your lips.<br> It has been like this for a long time.<br> Every time you fall asleep, you find yourself standing here.<br> You don’t remember coming here. You don’t know how or why it happens. Your dreams are peaceful; you never see the door within them or feel compelled to walk or run.<br> Your dreams have been your escape for so long, but now they feel like a trap.<br> You have taken to avoiding sleep for as long as possible, for though it is your only reprieve from the spectral doors that torment you throughout the Astral Kite, it brings you here, to the *real* door, night after night after night.<br> You have tried putting up surveillance cameras throughout the ship and watching the footage. Every time, it is the same. You see yourself sleep restfully for several hours, before rising to your feet and walking calmly through the ship’s corridors, directly to the closet door that leads down to stone columns and mahogany.<br> You have tried barricading yourself in your room, but it makes no difference. Watching the footage back, you see yourself calmly rise and remove the barricade before continuing on your way.<br> Asking the ship’s robots to barricade your door from the outside is no better. You watch yourself call the robots and override your previous order, ordering them to remove the barricade.<br> There is no stopping it.<br> Every day, your feet bring you to the mahogany door.<br> [Open the door]<pca1|<br> [Resolve never to sleep again]<pca2|<br> []<dispca1| (click: ?pca1)[(replace: ?pca2)[](replace:?dispca1)[There is no point in resisting further. You must sleep, and it is clear that you cannot resist this call forever.<br> You don’t recall asking your limbs to move, but your fingers caress the smooth grain of the door, the shining brass of the knocker.<br> The door swings open as you reach for the knob, as if it knew what you were going to do, as if all it ever needed was your permission.<br> And inside…<br> Inside, you peer inside, and—<br> You’re peering out into the corridor where you just stood, fluorescent lights flooding gray walls and narrow stairs.<br> *What is this*?<br> You glance behind you, over your shoulder, and see the same thing.<br> You step hesitantly forward, bare feet cold against the metal floor.<br> The mahogany door slams shut behind you. Trembling limbs carry you up the cramped stairs and through the closet door.<br> You find yourself in the empty corridors of the Astral Kite. Breath catching in your throat, you stumble back to your living quarters and collapse into your bed.<br> You don’t know how you fell asleep; you should have been too scared. But the bed is comfortable, and your body is tired, your limbs ache and cry for rest.<br> The rest, as ever, is peaceful.<br> And when, at last, you open your eyes—<br> You stare up at the ceiling of your room.<br> After a while, you get out of bed.<br> You walk through the ship’s corridors, to the bridge. You do not see the door anywhere.<br> It’s gone. You’re free.<br> Tears stream down your cheeks.<br> You’re free.<br> *But am I where I started? Or am I somewhere new? What would happen if I went back through the mahogany door*?<br> You resolve never to find out.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pca2)[(replace: ?pca1)[](replace:?dispca1)[Your fear is too overwhelming. You can’t bring yourself to touch the door.<br> You will do whatever it takes to avoid being forced here over and over, even if it means never sleeping again.<br> When you leave this place, the first thing you do is raid the medical bay and load your body full of stimulants.<br> It was already hard to keep track of time on this ship. Now, without sleep, maintained by a steady stream of stimulants, your body is ragged and timekeeping feels hopeless.<br> Hours pass like minutes, seconds pass like years.<br> You don’t recognize yourself when you look in the mirror. Your eyes are too bloodshot, your hair too unkempt, your face too sunken.<br> And though you do not sleep, you still see the spectral doors throughout the Astral Kite.<br> You blink, and sometimes, they disappear. But sometimes, the phantom doors appear as solid as the real one.<br> You continue like this, though you do not know for how long. But one day, perhaps it is years later, or perhaps it is the very day you first took the stimulants, as you return to the medical bay for your next dose, you find, to your horror, that the entry is blocked by a phantom door, shadowy mahogany sucking the life out of the hall and your lungs alike.<br> You collapse to your knees.<br> There, on the floor, you can feel your eyes starting to close.<br> No.<br> If you let yourself sleep now, your feet will carry you back down to the real door.<br> You can’t bear it.<br> You stagger back to your feet, eyes bleary.<br> It’s alright, you tell yourself. This is a phantom door, not the real one.<br> You can’t even blink, now, to make it go away. If you let your eyes close, you’re afraid you’ll fall asleep, even just for a moment.<br> No.<br> The door sways before you, or perhaps it is your legs that sway beneath you.<br> You can do this. It’s alright. This isn’t the real door.<br> You need another dose of stimulants, or you are going to collapse here and now. And then you *will* be faced with the real door.<br> Taking a halting breath in, you charge forward. There’s a crash as you trip, flying into the medical bay.<br> You see it coming as you fall. It doesn’t matter. Your limbs are too tired to stop it. Your head hits the corner of a metal table. You don’t feel anything.<br> Your eyes are closed.<br> Your eyes open, and you gasp, only—<br> The door is not here. The door is not here!<br> You are…you are on the floor.<br> You slept. You slept! And your feet did not carry you anywhere.<br> You could almost laugh with joy, except you can’t seem to make yourself move at all.<br> Your fingers twitch. There’s something wet and sticky on your ear.<br> *Oh*. You realize, then, what has happened. <br> You try to move, to call out, but it’s no use.<br> Then the door *does* appear again, hovering against the ceiling above you.<br> It swings open.<br> You squeeze your eyes closed.<br> They never open again.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] ] (else:)[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} Everywhere you turn, now, you see these phantom doors. It has become almost impossible to walk through the halls of the Astral Kite, so filled are they with the haunting imagery of mahogany, brass, and stone.<br> Every last amenity flickers behind the dark, smooth grain of the door. Every entryway in the ship is adorned with the shimmering specter of a brass knocker.<br> You would hardly leave your bed, except that your living quarters, too, are beset with stone columns, rough and drab, sometimes accosting the foot of your bed, or the door to the room.<br> There is no escape. In the end, you know this madness must stop. And you know what you must do.<br> [Destroy the mahogany door]<pcb1|<br> [Lock yourself on the bridge of the ship]<pcb2|<br> []<dispcb1| (click: ?pcb1)[(replace: ?pcb2)[](replace:?dispcb1)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} There is not much in the way of destructive weaponry aboard the Astral Kite. Even if Celestial Streams had been more thorough in their psychological tests before sending you out here alone, you do not think they would have left axes or a flamethrower lying around the ship. It didn’t seem prudent for a journey such as this one, subjected to such long bouts of loneliness.<br> But that does not mean you haven’t had a long time, sufficient time, to explore this ship and find ample materials to construct your own improvised armament.<br> Dodging phantom doors, you gather everything you need, spending hours sharpening the largest knife in the kitchen, and days jury-rigging spare gas canisters with oil fuel flasks until you have a makeshift flamethrower.<br> Then, you march through the corridors of the ship, down the claustrophobic stairs to the fluorescent hallway, and at last face to face with the mahogany door itself. Not a phantom, but the *real* door. Stone columns rising in shadow, the door consuming all light that comes near, except for the odd rays that glimmer off the brass knocker and doorknob.<br> You waste no time. You splash the door with oil, then light your flamethrower.<br> Alarms blare out the moment the door catches alight, radiance from the azure flames piercing the haunting shadows. You could swear you heard a scream.<br> As the ship’s alarms continue to ring out, detecting smoke and perhaps even the dangerous heat, you at last decide the fire has burned long enough and, setting aside your flamethrower to brandish a fire extinguisher stolen from the bridge, you put the fire out.<br> Smoke and chemical powder hangs in the air, suspended like a colloid solution in the fluorescent lighting.<br> When at last the smoke and powder clear, you see the door appears…<br> Unharmed.<br> Grimacing, you stride forward and strike it with your sharpened knife, digging into the wood point-first.<br> It bounces off the door, sparking like metal struck against metal.<br> Gritting your teeth, you try striking it again and again, lashing out with greater and greater abandon.<br> You hiss as the knife rebounds from the stubborn door, slips in your grasp, and nicks one of your fingers. Blood coats the blade and your clothes alike, and you pause to examine the injury. It’s deep, but the finger is in no danger of being lost. It may require stitches, but you can handle that in the medical bay later.<br> For now, you tear a piece of cloth from the bottom of your shirt and wrap it tight around the finger. Blood soaks through it almost immediately, but your attention is already back on the door.<br> Furious, you swing your knife one more time.<br> This time, it buries deep in the wood.<br> You gasp. The blood appears to seep from the knife into the door, running in rivulets down the grain.<br> You strike it again. And again. And again.<br> The bloody blade carves through the door, leaving gaping wounds that bleed and bleed, and it seems like too much blood to have originated only from the knife. But, then again, your finger is still bleeding quite a lot. Your makeshift cloth bandage is soaked through and blood is trickling down your wrist, your elbow, dripping to the floor below.<br> You’ll be alright.<br> You stab the door again.<br> It takes you several minutes, but the door chips apart, splinters unraveling, until at last, the brass of the knocker falls with an echoing clatter to the floor, and the doorknob not long after it.<br> Behind the fallen door, you see…nothing.<br> There’s nothing.<br> Just an empty wall, no different from the other walls of the spaceship.<br> And when you look again, every last splinter of the wood is gone. There is only the stain of your blood dripping down the metal wall, smeared by the blade of the knife, and pooling on the floor below.<br> You stand there for a long moment.<br> Then you walk back up the stairs, toward the medical bay.<br> You’ll be alright. And the door is gone.<br> Here, and all throughout the ship, the door is gone.<br> You smile. Now you’ll be able to jog through the ship again in peace.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pcb2)[(replace: ?pcb1)[](replace:?dispcb1)[You long for the days that you could jog. Your limbs itch with restlessness, but you have no choice. You cannot risk it, not with all the phantom doors blocking every corridor.<br> No, your only option is to make one place your home, to make it yours, and to fortify it, and never to leave.<br> There is only one room on the ship that is *required* for the mission, and that is the bridge. Your living quarters are more comfortable, but you can sleep on the bridge. You can call for the ship’s robots to bring you food and water. There’s even a small lavatory attached to the bridge. You will never have to leave.<br> There are wraiths here, too, of course. Apparitions of mahogany in the shadows, glimmers of brass on the control panel, rough gray stone morphing onto sleek gray walls.<br> But you cannot be forced to move through those terrible images, here, if you are not forced to move.<br> And for a time, the plan works. You are able to make this small space your home, and though the doors never leave you, you do not have to interact with them. They simply hover, for a time, here and then gone.<br> Until, one day, you awaken on the cot you’ve fashioned for yourself on the floor of the bridge to find yourself surrounded. Hovering just above your nose is the murky umber of the wood, the brass knocker hanging between your eyes, the doorknob loitering just above your elbow. Your arms are pinned tight to your body to avoid the stone columns, which rest against the floor beside you.<br> There is no escape.<br> [Wait. This is all part of the plan. Just wait.]<pcbi|<br> [Enough. The doors aren’t real. Get up.]<pcbii|<br> []<dispcbi| (click: ?pcbi)[(replace: ?pcbii)[](replace:?dispcbi)[You do not move a muscle. You refuse.<br> Minutes pass, and the door does not move.<br> It is alright. It is all part of the plan. You have made this place your fortress, and you will not retreat from it.<br> Minutes turn to hours, and the door does not move.<br> It is alright. You are more stubborn than this door knows.<br> Hours pass, and you wonder if days pass. Without moving, you have no way of knowing. You grow weary of counting the whorls in the grain of the door, cataloging the grooves in the stone of the columns.<br> It is alright. This door will go, sooner or later. They always do.<br> Your limbs grow weary. You sleep, and you wake, and the door is still here, imprisoning you.<br> It is alright. You will outlast it. It is a battle of wills, and your will is stronger.<br> You lose count of how many times you sleep and awaken, but every time, the door is still there.<br> Your lips grow dry and cracked. Your voice turns to a rasping whisper.<br> You decide to call for help; perhaps one of the ship’s robots can break through the phantom door. Perhaps they can deliver food and water to you, and in the process, destroy your illusory prison.<br> You certainly can’t risk touching the door yourself.<br> Perhaps your voice is too quiet, and the robots cannot hear you. You attempt to yell, but your throat is too dry. All that comes out is a raspy sigh.<br> It is alright. It is alright.<br> When next you open your eyes, you know you are running out of time. You will have to move soon.<br> Still, the door keeps you trapped. There is no room to move, and your fear chains you even tighter. You cannot bring yourself to risk touching the door.<br> You cannot bring yourself to sit upright.<br> When next you open your eyes, you know that you couldn’t sit upright even if you wanted to. You are too weak.<br> The brass flickers in your eyes. You wonder what light could possibly be catching the lustrous material, with the door blocking everything else in the bridge from view.<br> You almost summon the willpower to lift a finger to touch it. Almost.<br> When next you open your eyes, it is only for a few moments, a few breaths.<br> And then, you do not open them again.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?pcbii)[(replace: ?pcbi)[](replace:?dispcbi)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'playwhenpossible')} Taking a deep breath, steeling every nerve in your body, squeezing your eyes closed and tensing your muscles, you sit up.<br> Nothing happens. You open your eyes.<br> The door is gone. In fact, all the doors are gone.<br> Of course they are, you think. They weren’t real.<br> Just a figment of your imagination, an imagination running unchecked in the isolated madness of deep space, and a lengthy journey undertaken alone.<br> For the first time in a long time, you step out of the bridge.<br> You wander the corridors of the ship for hours, and still, there are no spectral doors.<br> Finally, you come to the narrow stairs and the fluorescent hall. And here, at the bottom of the stairs, you cannot stop yourself from looking.<br> There are two rough stone columns standing on either side of a mahogany door enshrouded in darkness, with a brass knocker and a brass doorknob.<br> The door is real. At least, *this* door is real.<br> You step forward, now, with little hesitation, and touch it. The wood is cool, smooth and polished. The brass warms to the touch.<br> The door opens when you turn the knob.<br> Behind it is…a small closet, stashed with boxes of books. You dig through the titles; it’s all science fiction and fantasy. Authors you love, too: Ursula K. Le Guin, Octavia Butler, Tasha Suri, RF Kuang, CL Clarke, and many, many more.<br> You laugh, then. You’re not sure whether you’re laughing at your foolishness, or as an incongruous response to your fear that you came so close to losing yourself, or out of joy for finding this stash of books. But you laugh, and you sit there, and you grab a book to read.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] ] } </div></div><div class="weights-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $correct to "") (set: $stopshutdown to "False") (set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt8 have passed for you on the ship.<br> A couple of incidents, no matter how dangerous, were never going to stop you from working out. (if: $weights3 is "3A")[You’ve long settled into a routine of makeshift weights in the cabin, no special exercise room necessary.]<br> But today, as you go to lift some weights, the lights throughout the entire ship abruptly shut off, leaving you in complete darkness.<br> Then, faint red auxiliary lights illuminate the edges of the walls, and a loud automated voice begins speaking over the intercoms. “Emergency shutdown sequence initiated. All systems will be shut down in 120 seconds. 119. 118…”<br> [Run to the bridge to override the shutdown!]<p1|<br> [Two minutes is plenty of time. Finish your current set of bicep curls, then go override the shutdown.]<p2|<br> [Enough is enough. Let it go.]<p3|<br> []<disp4| (click: ?p1)[(replace: ?p2)[](replace: ?p3)[](replace:?disp4)[You sprint to the bridge, pulling up a computer terminal.<br> The ship’s AI immediately shuts the terminal down.<br> “103. 102. 101...”<br> Cursing, you move to a second terminal and swiftly type a command to shut down the ship’s AI. The AI shuts the terminal down before you can complete it.<br> Cursing again, you go to the third and final computer terminal on the bridge, initiating a restart sequence. There will be a few moments when the computer turns back on, before the AI is fully up and running.<br> “77. 76. 75…”<br> Your heart is pounding. The screen comes back on.<br> You enter the command to shut down the ship AI.<br> It goes through.<br> “70. 69. 68…”<br> Then, with the ship’s AI off, you enter the command to override the shutdown.<br> The countdown stops.<br> Relief floods your limbs, and you let out a long, heavy breath.<br> Then, you spend hours digging through the ship’s code.<br> This couldn’t be an accident. Someone must have done this.<br> It takes you a long time to find, but here, on this journey, you have nothing *but* time.<br> And the conclusion is inescapable.<br> Celestial Streams built in a number of kill-switches, to be activated automatically once you reached a certain distance from Earth.<br> The first was the oxygen leak; for some reason, they didn’t expect you to catch such a leak in the exercise room. They must have thought you wouldn’t spend nearly as much time exercising.<br> The next was the thermometer breaking. Just like the oxygen leak, it was built into the code. A planned failure point, causing the readings to go wrong, with the goal of killing you via heat stroke while also burning up the ship’s energy, so that even if you stopped it, the Astral Kite might not have the power to continue its journey.<br> It was some combination of luck and your own quick smarts that saved you.<br> These were clearly built-in to appear as accidents. Mistakes that would allow them to make you into a martyr of the first interstellar mission.<br> The final kill-switch, however, contained very little in the way of such deception. Perhaps, though, you’re far enough out, and you come up so little in the typical news cycle, that an emergency shutdown could be reported as little more than a strange accident in deep space. One, perhaps, with an air of mystery.<br> Celestial Streams never wanted you to survive this mission. They wanted you alive just long enough to profit off of you, but by the time you reached your destination, you would have depreciated in value. By then, surely, other academic corporations will have followed suit.<br> But if they could make you a martyr, if they could kill you off long before you reached your destination, perhaps then they could continue to profit off of you on a more meaningful timescale.<br> You’re horrified.<br> But you’ve stopped them.<br> You’re still alive.<br> Despite everything, you’re still alive.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?p2)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace: ?p3)[](replace:?disp4)[You finish your reps on each side, heaving with exhaustion, and set the weights down.<br> “75. 74. 73. 72. 71…”<br> Catching your breath, you jog over to the bridge, pulling up a computer terminal.<br> The ship’s AI immediately shuts the terminal down.<br> “49. 48. 47…”<br> Oh, no.<br> Cursing, you move to a second terminal and swiftly type a command to shut down the ship’s AI. The AI shuts the terminal down before you can complete it.<br> Cursing again, you go to the third and final computer terminal on the bridge, initiating a restart sequence. There will be a few moments when the computer turns back on, before the AI is fully up and running.<br> “23. 22. 21…”<br> Is there going to be enough time??<br> You don’t know. It’s going to be close.<br> Your heart is pounding. The screen comes back on.<br> (link-replace: "Enter shutdown sequence")[(show: ?down)]<countdown| |down)[{(set: $counter to 10) (replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining]<br> (live: 0.5s)[ (stop:) [S]<s| (click: ?s)[(set: $correct to it + "s")(replace: ?s)[S]]] (live: 1s)[ (stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining]] (live: 2s)[ (stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining]] (live: 2.1s)[(stop:) [H]<h| (click: ?h)[(set: $correct to it + "h")(replace: ?h)[H]]] (live: 3s)[ (stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining]] (live: 3.5s)[(stop:) [U]<u| (click: ?u)[(set: $correct to it + "u")(replace: ?u)[U]]] (live: 4s)[ (stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining]] (live: 4.5s)[(stop:) [T]<t| (click: ?t)[(set: $correct to it + "t")(replace: ?t)[T]]] (live: 5s)[(stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining] ] (live: 5.5s)[(stop:) [D]<d| (click: ?d)[(set: $correct to it + "d")(replace: ?d)[D]]] (live: 6s)[ (stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining] ] (live: 6.5s)[(stop:) [O]<o| (click: ?o)[(set: $correct to it + "o")(replace: ?o)[O]]] (live: 7s)[ (stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining] ] (live: 7.5s)[(stop:) [W]<w| (click: ?w)[(set: $correct to it + "w")(replace: ?w)[W]]] (live: 8s)[ (stop:) (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining] ] (live: 8.5s)[(stop:) [N]<n| (click: ?n)[(set: $correct to $correct + "n")(replace: ?n)[N]]] (live: 8.75s)[ (stop:)[Shutdown]<sd|<br> (click: ?sd)[(set: $stopshutdown to "True")] ] (live: 9s)[ (stop:) (if: $correct is "shutdown" and $stopshutdown is "True")[] (else:)[ (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter second remaining]] ] (live: 10s)[ (stop:) (if: $correct is "shutdown" and $stopshutdown is "True")[] (else:)[ (set: $counter to it - 1)(replace: ?countdown)[$counter seconds remaining]] (show: ?shut)] }] |shut)[(if: $correct is "shutdown" and $stopshutdown is "True")[{(track: 'cycle56-action', 'pause') (track: 'working-out', 'loop', true) (track: 'working-out', 'playwhenpossible')} (if: $counter is 1)[(set: $remaining to (string: $counter) + " second remaining")] (else:)[(set: $remaining to (string: $counter) + " seconds remaining")] The countdown stops. You glance at the clock. //$remaining//<br> Relief floods your limbs, and you let out a long, heavy breath.<br> You want to look into this disaster, but in all honesty, you’re too tired.<br> Suffice to say that someone wanted you dead. Who knows why?<br> Whatever reason, they are far, far away from you now.<br> And you’re well past halfway to your destination; in truth, you’re nearing the end of the journey.<br> It doesn’t matter.<br> All that matters is that you’ve survived.<br> You’ve made it.<br> You’re going to make it.<br> Whew. Your heart is still beating fast. That was a workout.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]] (else:)[ (if: $correct is "shutdown")[You are too slow.<br>] (else-if: $stopshutdown is "True")[You have failed to enter the shutdown sequence correctly.<br>] “3. 2. 1.”<br> There is a moment of silence as all lights in the Astral Kite go out, even the auxiliary red lights.<br> Then, a heavier silence, as all systems shut down. It’s strange, realizing how much noise there really was on this ship. It always felt quiet, but not like this.<br> Then, a loud groaning sound pierces the silence.<br> Out of the corner of your eye, you see the metal paneling curling away from the cabin wall like a banana peel, or the plastic wrapped around a bandage.<br> You feel it at the same time you hear it; the scream of vacuum, of air being sucked through the hole in the ship. Your body flying toward eternity in the instantaneously depressurized cabin.<br> [[The End->Credits]]] ] ]] (click: ?p3)[(replace: ?p1)[](replace: ?p2)[](replace:?disp4)[You’ve had enough of running around putting out fires, out here.<br> There’s nothing more for you to do on this journey. You stopped caring about reaching your destination a long time ago.<br> “81. 80. 79…”<br> If this is how it’s going to end, so be it.<br> Standing here, waiting, you wonder who caused this. Between the oxygen leak, the temperature controls failing, and now this shutdown sequence, someone must want you dead.<br> Was it Celestial Streams? Did they want you dead for some specific reason? Or maybe there was a disgruntled employee there?<br> “44. 43. 42…”<br> Or, perhaps, was it one of the company’s rivals? Was it another corporation who wanted to destroy Celestial Streams’ reputation?<br> Perhaps, it was even someone who resented you for having this opportunity to be the first person to achieve interstellar travel. Perhaps there was someone out there who wanted to be the first to travel to another star system themselves, and they were determined not to let you reach your destination.<br> “26. 25. 24…”<br> There’s no way to know.<br> And, frankly, at this point, you don’t care.<br> “18. 17. 16…”<br> You close your eyes, taking deep breaths, letting the numbers wash over you.<br> “10. 9. 8…”<br> It’s okay. It’s okay.<br> “5. 4. 3….”<br> You take another breath.<br> “2. 1.”<br> You breathe out, and open your eyes.<br> The auxiliary lights turn off, leaving you in complete darkness.<br> You hear a loud banging sound, like metal tearing apart.<br> And then…<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] } </div></div><div class="horror-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt8 have passed for you on the ship.<br> You have forgotten how to differentiate the sound of your own breath from the hum of the Astral Kite. You can no longer make out the place where your shadow ends and the crevices of the cabin wall begin. Sometimes, you are incapable of leaving your room. Sometimes, you run screaming, or crying, or laughing, or some combination of all three through the corridors, if for no other reason than you have nothing better to do.<br> After all, there’s no one else here to see you. You have no community, no support, no family, no friends. There’s nothing to give you continuity.<br> Sometimes, there are voices, though often they seem to be your own. Sometimes, you find things written down places, papers scattered through the cabin, more graffiti spread over the walls, handwriting you recognize even if you do not remember the act of writing it.<br> But do you recognize the handwriting because it’s yours? Or simply because you’ve seen it before?<br> There’s nothing here, you remind yourself. The vacuum of space, the empty corridors, and you.<br> There’s no one here with you. There’s no one else. No one.<br> Right?<br> [Right.]<pA|<br> [Wrong!]<pB|<br> []<disp1| (click: ?pA)[(replace: ?pB)[](replace:?disp1)[Yes, you are alone. You and the Universe and no one else. Humans. People. Earth. These words are nothing but a collection of sounds. They carry no significance for you any longer.<br> You come back to yourself, finding your face pressed up against the viewport of the Astral Kite’s bridge, begging for a change, for something new, for anything that could give your life contrast. Something capable of imparting *meaning*.<br> There is nothing.<br> You find yourself looking at your reflection in the viewport. You haven’t looked at your reflection in a while. You find it’s quite different from how you remember yourself.<br> You trace new wrinkles with your fingers. Your hair is longer than it’s ever been, and you spend ages examining it, playing with it, fascinated. Your eyes—have they always been that color? They must have. But they seem brighter than you remember, even if they are slightly sunken.<br> You have changed. *You* have changed.<br> You sit back down in the cabin, shocked. Your thoughts have slowed, calmed.<br> You don’t quite understand how, or why, but you know that this changes things.<br> You are alone, but that does not stop you from being.<br> This lonely, horrific existence is not the life you saw for yourself, but it does not stop you from existing.<br> It does not stop time from passing.<br> It may not always make sense, but you are still here.<br> You are still here.<br> You are still here.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pB)[(replace: ?pA)[](replace:?disp1)[Haha! Wrong! There’s someone else here, and you’ve known it the whole time. You’ve been on to them practically since launch!<br> They’ve stayed hidden for far too long…It’s time to root them out!<br> All your fears forgotten, you dig through every last centimeter of the Astral Kite. There is no panel that goes unturned, no door left unopened, no closet left unexamined. You cover everything, systematically, scientifically; this might be the most scientific thing you’ve done since you got here.<br> It takes ages (time still has no meaning for you, so you’re not sure exactly how long; you can’t risk letting the stowaway slip through your grasp, so you don’t sleep, but that doesn’t mean much), and when you’re done, you can’t quite believe it.<br> You didn’t find anyone. They must have dodged you, somehow. You didn’t think it was possible. You planned it all so carefully to prevent it. You enlisted the help of the robots on board. It doesn’t matter.<br> You try again.<br> The same result. Nothing. No one.<br> It’s your third try—no, your fourth—when you catch a glimpse of something (someone?) in the mirror in the ship’s restroom.<br> You gasp, turning and sprinting into the cabin, where they’ll have nowhere to hide.<br> Your eyes dart around the barren metal room. There’s nothing. No one.<br> Are you just seeing things? Imagining things?<br> Well, are you?<br> [Yes]<pb1|<br> [No]<pb2|<br> []<dispb| (click: ?pb1)[(replace: ?pb2)[](replace:?dispb)[Yes…Yes, you are. It’s a difficult thing to accept, but it is long past time you accepted it.<br> You shudder and sit down on the cold metal floor of the ship.<br> People were not meant for this. That is your solace. The knowledge that what you are going through is something people were not meant to experience, something that was not made for you, something you have not evolved to deal with very well.<br> You feel like you should be crying, but your cheeks are strangely dry.<br> It’s alright, you tell yourself. Things are not alright, but there’s nothing to be done, now. It’s too late. And that’s alright.<br> When you look back at this moment, you will see it less as a moment of revelation, and more as a moment of acceptance.<br> It’s alright.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pb2)[(replace: ?pb1)[](replace:?dispb)[(set: $alone to "False")No, you beg. No, no, no! There must be someone else here!<br> There must!<br> The alternative is too ghastly, too terrifying to face. Not what it means for your sanity up to this point, for what you’ve imagined, the voices you’ve heard, the writing you’ve seen on the wall, the things you must have done and forgotten, but for what it means for your sanity going forward.<br> You cannot imagine the rest of this journey alone.
