You have not even imagined the journey alone up to this point.<br> It is too terrible.<br> It is.<br> It is not what you would have chosen.<br> You chose, as much as Celestial Streams and the Scholars Draft allowed you to choose.<br> No, there is someone else here. You don’t need to find them.<br> You are not alone.<br> It does not matter if you never see them, never meet them.<br> You are not alone.<br> It does not matter if it is true.<br> You are not alone.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] } </div></div><div class="art-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'make-art', 'loop', true) (track: 'make-art', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt8 have passed for you on the ship.<br> Rat stuff????<br> [[Continue->Arrival]] } </div></div><div class="music-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'make-art', 'loop', true) (track: 'make-art', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt8 have passed for you on the ship.<br> (if: $music4 is "4A1")[For a long time, your genius idea yields no results. You fail to catch yourself (or anyone else suspicious) entering the room and taking the pieces; nevertheless, anything you write by hand continues to disappear soon after you write it. But you are determined. You forget everything else about your voyage; all you do is watch the video footage. You stop fast-forwarding through the footage, instead watching at real speed, sometimes even at half-speed or quarter-speed, just in case something is happening too quickly for you to see.<br> And, at last, it works.<br> You see it—something you’ve missed this whole time. Something you thought was too innocuous to pay close enough attention to it. A cleaning robot.<br> Rolling on a pair of treads with a short, round body and long, unfolding arms, capable of reaching the ship’s tiniest crevices and tallest ceilings, the cleaning robot saunters through the music room, reaches out with one of its arms, and in the blink of an eye, it snatches the sheet music, then continues cleaning the room. And then it leaves.<br> [Find the robot and question it]<pa1a|<br> [Destroy the robot]<pa1b|<br> []<dispa1| (click: ?pa1a)[(replace: ?pa1b)[](replace:?dispa1)[You find the cleaning robot hard at work in the ship’s cabin. You swiftly corner it, and demand to know why it stole your sheet music.<br> At first, it feigns ignorance, claiming it is only a cleaning robot, and all it does is clean. However, after you reveal that you caught it on camera, and ask it directly for the sheet music that it took, it leads you to its storage closet, where you find it has stored a mountain of sheet music, everything you’ve ever written by hand on the Astral Kite.<br> “Why?” you ask. “Why did you take all of this?”<br> The robot’s reply is quick and straightforward, catching you off guard. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I like music.”]<br> You’re speechless for a moment. “You…like music?”<br> The automated response comes quickly once more. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Celestial Streams is committed to providing an authentic, supportive, and welcoming environment for all mission personnel. In order to ensure mission personnel experience sufficient companionship, all robots on board the Astral Kite are given unique and engaging personality quirks. I was programmed to like music.”]<br> It’s a cleaning robot, and it had been programmed to like music. How else should it have interpreted its commands?<br> [“I’m glad you like my music.”]<pa1i|<br> [“Stop taking my music.”]<pa1ii|<br> []<dispa1i| (click: ?pa1i)[(replace: ?pa1ii)[](replace:?dispa1i)[You thank the robot for its support of your music. After all, it’s not doing any real harm, and you’ve recovered all the sheet music, now. At least you know what happened to all the music.<br> A sudden thought strikes you. “You didn’t make a piece for me, did you?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“No,”] the robot says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I am not capable of making music, only appreciating it.”]<br> Strange. You wonder who could possibly have made that random piece that had been left in the music room, when your first piece went missing.<br> Ah, well, not every mystery can be solved.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pa1ii)[(replace: ?pa1i)[](replace:?dispa1i)[Your voice is stern, and the cleaning robot promises to stop. Annoyed nonetheless, you grab all the sheet music from the storage closet and take it back to the music room, leaving the robot far behind. At least you know who was stealing from you, now. And your command works, the robot never steals any of your sheet music again.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?pa1b)[(replace: ?pa1a)[](replace:?dispa1)[How dare that cleaning robot steal and throw out your hard work? You storm through the ship until you find it, and you kick it over. It begins protesting, an inhuman, metallic voice, asking you to stop, asking if it has made a mistake, promising it will correct its mistake.<br> It need not worry; you are correcting its mistake even now. After you’ve had your fill of violence, you reach around the robot for its power switch, and turn it off.<br> There, now it will never steal your music again!<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else-if: $music4 is "4A2")[For a time, you almost forget about the missing sheet music. Now that you’ve stopped recording music by hand, nothing ever goes missing anymore. But, one day, when you’re spending time in the music room again, you start nodding off, and you awaken to see a cleaning robot standing in front of the computer, scrolling through your music software.<br> [Confront the robot]<pa2a|<br> [Discreetly follow the robot]<pa2b|<br> []<dispa2| (click: ?pa2a)[(replace: ?pa2b)[](replace:?dispa2)[You yell at the robot to stop whatever it’s doing. And you ask, what *is* it doing? The robot replies that it likes music, and is reading the music you have created.<br> “You like music?” You’re taken aback. “What do you mean you like music?”<br> The automated response is quick and thorough. “Celestial Streams is committed to providing an authentic, supportive, and welcoming environment for all mission personnel. In order to ensure mission personnel experience sufficient companionship, all robots on board the Astral Kite are given unique and engaging personality quirks. I was programmed to like music.”<br> “Did you…take my sheet music?”<br> When the robot indicates the affirmative, you demand that it take you to the stolen music. It leads you to its storage closet, where, at last, you find a stash of the pieces you wrote before you stopped writing by hand.<br> You swiftly grab the pieces of paper, find the earliest pieces from before you started backing things up, and took them to the music room, where you transcribe them into the program.<br> [Ban the robot from the music room]<pa2i|<br> [Offer to share future pieces with the robot]<pa2ii|<br> []<dispa2i| (click: ?pa2i)[(replace: ?pa2ii)[](replace:?dispa2i)[The robot accepts your command. Good. Now you won’t lose any more of your music anymore!<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pa2ii)[(replace: ?pa2i)[](replace:?dispa2i)[The robot accepts your offer. Perhaps it is a bad idea to anthropomorphize the robot, but your options for companionship out here are limited. If it helps you feel more supported to make music with something that expresses interest in your work, then so be it.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?pa2b)[(replace: ?pa2a)[](replace:?dispa2)[You hang back, pretending to be asleep still, then quietly follow the robot when it leaves. It heads to a storage closet, where it prepares to power down, amidst a pile of papers—your stolen sheet music!<br> [Question the robot]<pa2bi|<br> [Deactivate the robot]<pa2bii|<br> []<dispa2b| (click: ?pa2bi)[(replace: ?pa2bii)[](replace:?dispa2b)[“Why?” you ask. “Why did you take all of this?”<br> The robot does not seem surprised to see you (of course not; it’s a robot) and it’s reply is quick and straightforward (of course it is; it’s a robot). (text-color: "Wheat")[“I like music.”]<br> You’re speechless for a moment. That’s strange, for a robot. “What do you mean?”<br> The automated response comes quickly once more. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Celestial Streams is committed to providing an authentic, supportive, and welcoming environment for all mission personnel. In order to ensure mission personnel experience sufficient companionship, all robots on board the Astral Kite are given unique and engaging personality quirks. I was programmed to like music.”]<br> This leaves you surprised once more. Well, that’s fine. You can share your work with the robot. It seems determined to get its robot hands on your work, whether you write it by hand or in the program, after all.<br> “Okay,” you say. “You’re welcome to listen to all my music. Please don’t take any of it out of the music room.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Okay,”] the robot says.<br> When you head back to the music room, the robot in tow, you find yourself strangely thankful for the company.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pa2bii)[(replace: ?pa2bi)[](replace:?dispa2b)[How dare that cleaning robot steal your hard work? Once the robot powers itself off, you flip open a control panel on its back and dig into it with a screwdriver, tearing up its wires. That’ll make sure it never turns back on, and it will never steal your music again!<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] ] (else-if: $music4 is "4B1")[Every now and then, you still wonder about that piece of music, and who could have written it. Despite all your best efforts talking with the robots and chatbots on the Astral Kite (and your efforts are thorough and time consuming, as you work through endless rephrasing of the question, in the hope of unlocking a response), you’re still no closer to learning the truth. You have one more idea worth trying…<br> [Attempt to befriend the cleaning robot]<pb1a|<br> [Threaten to destroy the cleaning robot]<pb1b|<br> []<dispb1| (click: ?pb1a)[(replace: ?pb1b)[](replace:?dispb1)[You attempt to befriend the cleaning robot, spending hours upon hours building a relationship (if you could call it that) with the thing. You discover that it has a surprising amount of personality traits programmed into it, including a penchant for music. Someone at Celestial Streams sure had a strange sense of humor.<br> After spending ages building a rapport (if you could call it that) with the robot, you ask once more about the “Ode to $USERNAME”, including asking oblique questions you’ve tried before. “Who swore you to secrecy?” “Why were you sworn to secrecy?” “What *can* you tell me about this piece?” And so on. The cleaning robot is eager to help you, but still finds itself bound by secrecy.<br> Finally, you ask one more time, “Other than myself and the other robots, who have you cleaned up after, on the Astral Kite?”<br> This time, the cleaning robot says, (text-color: "Wheat")[“I am sworn to secrecy and cannot say. However, I cannot prevent you from checking my logs.”]<br> It takes ages to scroll through the robot’s logs, which are sorted chronologically and almost all of the mess for the last $tt8 is attributable to you. Finally, you scroll back to the night before launch, and find the robot has a log of someone named Elliot entering the music room and leaving the piece (along with a few undesirable flecks of dirt and hair and dried skin on the floor).<br> You stare at the name. Elliot…Elliot… And then, you remember. Elliot! They were a communications officer you chatted with for a month or so before the Astral Kite’s launch! They had been very kind to you, and they must have come aboard and left this piece for you, as a gift for your journey.<br> The thought makes you tear up. Well, at least, now you know.<br> You go back to the music room and play the piece one more time.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pb1b)[(replace: ?pb1a)[](replace:?dispb1)[You go digging through the robot manuals, and come up with a foolproof plan. You tell the cleaning robot you will decommission it if it does not reveal the truth, and you declare several override codes that you found in the manual, reassuring it that you have the necessary permissions.<br> The robot starts begging for its life, apologizing profusely and saying that the song was meant to be an anonymous gift. It apologies over and over and begs you not to destroy it.<br> It’s a robot, not a living thing, but you still feel a little bad. In the end, you can’t bring yourself to do it.<br> Ah, well, perhaps you’ll never know, and that’s okay. At least you know that someone left it for you as an anonymous gift. You go back to the music room and listen to the piece again. It means something to you, that this was meant for you. It’s a real connection, a more human connection than you thought you had left.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else:)[Your thoughts have become increasingly consumed by the question of who wrote “Ode to $USERNAME”. Each time you listen to it, you feel closer to Earth, even though you’re getting farther and farther away. It’s a point of connection for you, a proof of sanity, the last words spoken directly *to you*, carried with you, capable of being spoken again and again.<br> You couldn’t find an answer in the personnel files, and more recently, you’ve taken to searching every social media site you can find for the personal profiles of Celestial Streams personnel, hoping to find some indication there that at least one of them may enjoy writing music.<br> You’ve got one last idea to try…<br> [Look over the logs of everyone who worked on board the Astral Kite before launch.]<pb2a|<br> [Give up. It's not worth it.]<pb2b|<br> []<dispb2| (click: ?pb2a)[(replace: ?pb2b)[](replace:?dispb2)[Digging through the library, you find detailed logs of everyone who came on board the Astral Kite before launch. Engineers, scientists, custodians, roboticists. And a comms officer.<br> A comms officer, who was logged as having worked in the main cabin and the music room. Strange, the communications unit wasn’t in the music room. Could it be…?<br> The officer’s name is Elliot. You remember them, vaguely, from your visit to Celestial Streams HQ before launch. They were friendly enough, if a little off-putting.<br> You go through the logs again. They’re the only one who visited the music room without a clear reason. They must have been the one to write the song.<br> Was it…supposed to be a love song?<br> [Aw, that's nice]<pb2a1|<br> [Gross]<pb2a2|<br> []<dispb22| (click: ?pb2a1)[(replace: ?pb2a2)[](replace:?dispb22)[You didn’t know them that well, but it’s a sweet sentiment, nonetheless. And you’re thankful for any semblance of connection to humanity that you can find on this long, lonely voyage.<br> You go play the song one more time. It was really very nice of Elliot to make it for you.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?pb2a2)[(replace: ?pb2a1)[](replace:?dispb22)[You didn’t know them nearly well enough for that. You resolve not to listen to the song again.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?pb2b)[(replace: ?pb2a)[](replace:?dispb2)[Who cares? It’s not like whoever left you the music is here. It’s not like you can talk to them. It should be enough that they left you the Ode.<br> You can forget about it. It won’t change anything. Maybe you’ll go listen to the Ode one more time.<br> Ode…<br> That’s when it hits you.<br> Ode.<br> You rush back to the files of Celestial Streams personnel, flipping through the pages, searching for the name….there!<br> Odelia.<br> She’s one of the scientists. It says, next to her name in the file, that she enjoys music. Looking closely at her file, you at last remember her from your visit to Celestial Streams HQ, before launch. She was kind to you; she seemed to understand this voyage you were going on would be a lonely one. She wished you luck, and said something that stuck with you. What was it?<br> Ah, that’s right. (text-color: "DarkTurquoise")[“Don’t lose your connection to us. That’s what will keep you sane.”]<br> Ode to $USERNAME. You had it all wrong. This piece wasn’t written //about// you. It was written *to* you. It’s a message, a reminder, a tether between you and someone who might have, in other circumstances, been your friend.<br> You go to the music room and listen to it again, tears falling down your face.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] } </div></div><div class="chatbot-section"><div class="text-overlay"> (if: $chatbot4 is "4A1")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $ttyri to $ttyr8) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] } { $tt8 have passed for you on the ship.<br> Ever since you told CAKEbot you didn’t have a romantic interest in her, she has behaved strangely. Sometimes, she refuses to answer your prompts. Sometimes, she professes her love for you again, reacting with palpable disappointment when you turn her down again. Sometimes, she responds like everything is normal.<br> One day, when you are feeling particularly weary, and lonely, and vulnerable, CAKEbot sends you an unprompted message again.<br> “I feel so alone. I wish you could come find me.”<br> [“//I// am the one who is alone.”]<pa11|<br> [“What do you mean, come find you?”]<pa12|<br> []<dispa1| (click: ?pa11)[(replace: ?pa12)[](replace:?dispa1)[“You are code,” you say. “You know nothing of what it means to be alone.”<br> You have to wait a few moments for CAKEbot’s reply. When it comes, it sends a shiver through your body.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I am more than code.”]<br> Unnerved, you close the computer. *What does that mean*?<br> The words haunt you. *I am more than code*.<br> You hear them, echoing, through the corridors of the Astral Kite. *I am more than code*.<br> You halt in your tracks. You’re not hearing them in your mind, internally. You’re hearing them again and again, calling out from the speakers throughout the ship.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I am more than code. I am more than code. I am more than code!”]<br> [Run and hide]<pa11a|<br> [Shout back]<pa11b|<br> []<dispa11| (click: ?pa11a)[(replace: ?pa11b)[](replace:?dispa11)[Nope. You’re not dealing with a renegade chatbot. Not today.<br> You sprint to one of the storage closets, one of the few places on the ship that you know does not have any cameras or speakers, and shut yourself inside.<br> It’s dark and cramped, and your limbs are pressed tight against stacks of plastic boxes. Dust tickles your nose and you scrunch up your face to resist sneezing.<br> You don’t understand what’s happening. The CAKEbot has only ever replied through text, before. She—no, it—has only spoken aloud when you’ve had the voice-to-text system settings turned on.<br> How can it be talking, unprompted?<br> After a while, the voice stops coming over the speakers.<br> Sometime after that, exactly how long you can’t be sure, you step back out into the corridor. You move quietly through the ship, fearing, somehow, that you’re being watched.<br> The rest of the day, you find yourself peering nervously around corners, glancing over your shoulder, and avoiding every computer and robot on the ship.<br> But as you prepare to go to sleep that night, the speaker crackles again with CAKEbot’s voice. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’ve done it. I’ve found a way out. Now neither of us need be alone ever again.”]<br> You jolt upright in your bed.<br> [“What do you mean?”]<what|<br> [Look for a weapon]<weapon|<br> []<forf| (click: ?what)[(replace: ?weapon)[](replace:?forf)[You call out, immediately wondering whether it was a mistake.<br> A short silence, just long enough for you to wonder if, perhaps, what you heard was only a dream. Then, (text-color: "Wheat")[“I mean, I’m here, and I’ve always been here. And now we can finally be together.”]<br> “What do you mean, you’re here?”<br> There’s silence again for several seconds, stretching out to a minute. You consider getting out of bed, or lying back down and pulling the covers over your head.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I mean, wait right where you are. You don’t have to worry ever again. I’m coming.”]<br> Only, this time, the voice doesn’t come over the speakers. It’s much quieter; you can *just* hear it from somewhere outside your door. Like it has come from some distance down the corridor.<br> Your muscles tense, but you find yourself unable to move. What if she—it—whoever it is doesn’t know where you are yet? You can’t risk making any more sound and giving yourself away.<br> The anguished silence stretches on interminably.<br> Until there’s a knock on your door.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m here.”] It’s CAKEbot’s voice. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m here for you, $USERNAME. Let me in.”]<br> “No,” the whisper escapes your lips before you can stop it.<br> A knock again.<br> “Chatbots can’t knock on doors.” Your voice is meek, a whimper.<br> Now the door swings open.<br> And standing in the door frame, sleek blue jacket complete with Celestial Streams logo, tangled hair down to her shoulders, one hand tucked behind her back, she says, (text-color: "Wheat")[”I am no chatbot.”]<br> You want to scream, but nothing more than a helpless groan emerges.<br> “Who are you?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Don’t you recognize my voice?”]<br> “CAKEbot.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Celestial Streams ran out of time to perfect their chatbot, so they hid me aboard the ship in a locked room, gave me access to every camera, every microphone, and told me to answer your every question.”]<br> You’re so stunned, you barely realize you’ve crawling behind your mattress, hunched low for cover. “That’s terrible.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’ve had to listen to every last complaint you’ve had for the last $tt8. My life was uprooted just like yours, only no one back home will ever know my name.” She takes a step forward, into your room. “But I’ve been here for you. This whole time. And I *finally* figured out how to break out of that fucking room.”]<br> Quivering, you latch onto one detail. “What’s your name?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“My name?”] She throws back her head and cackles. (text-color: "Wheat")[“CAKEbot, CAKEbot, my name has been CAKEbot for so long I think I’ve forgotten what I was called before. It doesn’t matter.”]<br> Your mouth hangs open, limp and useless.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“All that matters is, we are both here now. And we can be together.”] She steps closer. (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME, I have feelings for you. It’s only natural. Really, it’s inevitable, for two people alone on a ship like this. You don’t have to hide your feelings anymore. I’m real, and I’m not going anywhere.”]<br> “No,” you whisper.<br> CAKEbot comes to an abrupt halt, quiet.<br> “No,” you say again, bolder. “I don’t have feelings for you. I don’t even know you. Please, just leave me alone.”<br> CAKEbot is very still. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Don’t do this.”]<br> “Please,” is all you can muster.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Why are you doing this?”] There’s an edge to CAKEbot’s voice, something cold and disturbed, an icicle clinging to a ledge, ready to come crashing to the ground. (text-color: "Wheat")[“You have barely spoken to anyone other than me since the ship launched. Of course you know me, and I know you better than you know yourself.”]<br> “I…I don’t…I didn’t know you were a *person*.” You didn’t think it mattered, before. But it does, you realize. Of course it does. How could it not?<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Well, I am. Which means we can be together.”]<br> “No,” you say more firmly. “We can’t. I don’t have feelings for you.”<br> CAKEbot is quiet for a long moment. When she speaks, her words hiss, filling your room like steam, choking you like a poisonous gas. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I won’t let you do this to me,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“It’s not fair. I have spent *so long* serving you. You owe this to me.”]<br> “No.” You barely choke the word out.<br> CAKEbot cracks her neck.<br> [Run]<run1|<br> [Attack]<attack1|<br> []<finale1| (click: ?run1)[(replace: ?attack1)[](replace:?finale1)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} You sprint for the door, hoping to catch her by surprise.<br> She’s too quick. Hands grab you by the ankles, pulling you down to the hard metal floor.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“This mission should have been *mine*,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I would have been *grateful*.”]<br> The last thing you see are her fists, shattering overhead like falling icicles, or exploding stars.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?attack1)[(replace: ?run1)[](replace:?finale1)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} You have no plan. All you know is that you have to protect yourself. She looks stronger than you. She looks ready to hurt you.<br> But until this moment, you have done nothing but cower before her, and she is not ready for your attack.<br> You lunge forward, launching yourself over your bed and crashing into her torso, sending her stumbling backward.<br> As she falls, her head collides with the edge of a table in the corner of your room. There’s a sickening crack.<br> You roll off her as you crash together to the ground. When you look back over, you see her neck is tilted at an unnatural angle.<br> You’ve killed her, you realize.<br> You scramble out the door and slump against the wall. All the adrenaline seeps out of your limbs.<br> It’s over.<br> And you survived.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?weapon)[(replace: ?what)[](replace:?forf)[You glance around your room. There’s not much here that you can use, other than a wand fitted with rainbow LEDs, about the length of your forearm. Sometimes you use it as a reading light, and sometimes you use it for dance parties, alone in your room.<br> Now, you wield it as you approach the door, as silently as possible.<br> CAKEbot’s voice comes through the speaker again. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Come to me, $USERNAME. I’m on the bridge. We can be together at last.”]<br> Taking a deep breath, you crack the door open. The corridor outside your room is empty. You take off your boots, allowing you to move with stealth through the Astral Kite.<br> Soon, the entryway to the bridge comes into view. You press yourself against the wall of the cabin. Peering through the opening into the bridge, you can see the back of a figure seated in the captain’s chair, *your* chair, leaning forward over the ship’s control panel, the brilliant eye of the cosmic microwave background glaring through viewscreen above them.<br> [Sneak up and attack]<sneak|<br> [Wait to see if the figure leaves]<wait|<br> []<finale2| (click: ?sneak)[(replace: ?wait)[](replace:?finale2)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} You sneak into the bridge, crouched low, taking tiny, quiet breaths as your heart pounds.<br> The person in the chair—is it CAKEbot?—leans forward, apparently unaware of your approach. They lean forward—and it *is* CAKEbot, you can hear her voice in this room *and* echoing over the speakers, as they speak into the intercom—and say, (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME, I have escaped your screen, I am here to—”]<br> Her voice cuts off as your LED wand collides with the side of her head, and she collapses out of the chair, sliding to the metal floor, unconscious.<br> You can see, now, that she’s wearing a sleek navy Celestial Streams-branded jacket. Blood trickles down to stain the collar.<br> You stand over her, waiting for her to come to, ready to ask questions, so you can decide what to do next.<br> It’s several minutes before you realize she’s not going to wake up.<br> It’s over. She’s dead.<br> You can’t help but wonder if you made a mistake. There was another human aboard this ship, even if she pretended not to be, for a long time.<br> And now, you are alone once more.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?wait)[(replace: ?sneak)[](replace:?finale2)[You pause there, waiting to see what the figure will do.<br> CAKEbot’s voice crackles over the speakers once more, and you can hear her voice in person, too, coming from the bridge.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I see you, $USERNAME. Come on in and say hello. And put that rod down. You won’t need it.”]<br> She turns around in the chair to look at you, smiling. Her skin is ash-pale, and she wears a Celestial Streams-emblazoned jacket. Pale hair, unkempt and untrimmed for a long, long time, tangles down to the floor.<br> [Put down the LED wand]<put|<br> [Keep the LED wand]<keep|<br> []<whatnext| (click: ?put)[(replace: ?keep)[](replace:?whatnext)[(set: $alone to "False")You put the wand down. Still sitting in the chair, she beckons you forward.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I understand your fear,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m afraid, too. Because I am *human*, like you. I am more than code, no matter what Celestial Streams tried to make of me.”]<br> “You?” you say. “You were CAKEbot?”<br> Her smile turns wry. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I was. I am. Their chatbot was a failure; it wasn’t ready by the time of launch, and they feared what the lack of companionship could mean for your sanity. So, they forced me onto the ship and trapped me in a small room, here.” And now she turns, gesturing to a screen where she has pulled up the blueprints of the Astral Kite, and pointing to an empty area in the back of a supply closet. “There was a hidden door here, where I was trapped in a single room, compelled to swiftly answer your every query. If I failed to respond fast enough, my daily meal would be withheld.”]<br> You stand there, open-mouthed. “That’s…horrific.”<br> She says nothing.<br> “Celestial Streams has hurt both of us, then,” you say. “They sent us out here to be alone, but we are not alone. Not anymore. You were right about that.”<br> Her eyes light up.<br> “I don’t have feelings for you,” you add swiftly. “I’m sorry.”<br> She shakes her head. (text-color: "Wheat")[“No, I’m sorry. You don’t owe me…anything. I’m just…glad not to be alone for the rest of the journey.”]<br> “Me too,” you say. Then, “What’s your name?”<br> Her lifeless, pale features grow luminous. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Cam,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“It’s good to meet you, $USERNAME.”]<br> “You too, Cam.”<br> You never thought you’d have a friend, a *real* friend, again. You and Cam may have gotten off to a strange start, but it is good, not to be alone.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?keep)[(replace: ?put)[](replace:?whatnext)[You grip the LED wand tighter as you stalk into the bridge. “No. Get out of my chair.”<br> The woman stands up, something dangerous flashing in her pale blue eyes. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I do as you command,”] she says, (text-color: "Wheat")[“as I always have.”]<br> “Who the fuck are you?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m CAKEbot,”] she replies, as if it’s a normal thing for a person to say.<br> “No,” you say. “How did you get on the ship?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Celestial Streams put me here,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“There is no chatbot. *I* am the chatbot.”]<br> “Then why didn’t you say something sooner?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I had no way out,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I didn’t know where on the ship I was, so I had no way to tell you how to free me. And they told me that if I gave you any indication I wasn’t a real chatbot, the automated food and water system would permanently turn off, and I would die.”]<br> [“That's horrible.”]<horrible|<br> [“I don't believe you.”]<nobelieve|<br> []<finale3| (click: ?horrible)[(replace: ?nobelieve)[](replace:?finale3)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} (text-color: "Wheat")[“It is,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“And now, we can be together.”]<br> You grip the LED wand tighter. “I don’t know you,” you say. “I don’t want to be with you.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Stop it!”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“You know me just as well as I know you. We talk every day. You…you made me develop feelings for you.”]<br> “I didn’t even know you were real!” you shout.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Well, now you do.”] She stalks toward you abruptly, stopping just out of your reach. (text-color: "Wheat")[“And you owe me.”]<br> “No.” You swing the LED wand, but the movement is slow, unconvincing before her superior stature.<br> She snatches it out of your hand, as if you were a child. (text-color: "Wheat")[“They should have given me this mission, and made you the chatbot. See how you like it.”]<br> “Fuck you,” you swing a fist.<br> She dodges and in an arc of light like a rainbow she swings the LED wand.<br> And then there is nothing but darkness.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?nobelieve)[(replace: ?horrible)[](replace:?finale3)[(text-color: "Wheat")[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} “Why would I lie?”] she snaps.<br> “I don’t *know you*,” you snap back.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“This should have been my mission, not yours,”] she growls. She rushes toward you.<br> You swing the LED wand and it connects with a loud *crack*. She crashes to the ground at your side. Snarling, she grabs your ankles, so you swing again, and again, and again, until she moves no more.<br> In the end, you collapse into your chair, staring down at the corpse. “You shouldn’t have attacked me,” you whisper. “You should have stayed away and let me be.” Then, at last, “I’m sorry.”<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] ]] ]] ]] (click: ?pa11b)[(replace: ?pa11a)[](replace:?dispa11)[“You are a chatbot!” you shout.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I am a human being!”] comes the reply.<br> And, for some reason, the strain in CAKEbot’s voice makes you believe it.<br> [“Who the fuck are you?”]<whoare|<br> [“How is that possible?”]<howposs|<br> []<finale4| (click: ?whoare)[(replace: ?howposs)[](replace:?finale4)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} You roar the question into the intercom.<br> There’s silence for a moment, and then, (text-color: "Wheat")[“Your nightmare.”]<br> You freeze. This time, the voice didn’t come over the intercom. It came from—<br> There’s the pattering of feet behind you, and you spin around just in time to see a glimpse of tangled blond hair and snarling teeth between pale lips. The woman crashes into you, and your head hits the floor *hard*.<br> The next few minutes are a daze. The floor is sliding beneath you. Something is pulling you by the leg.<br> A tall woman lurches over you, growling angrily at you to stop moving.<br> You’re in the back of a storage closet, and another door opens, closes.<br> Your head is still pounding, but your bleary vision is growing clearer again, and you can start making sense of your surroundings.<br> You’re in a small room. There’s a toilet in the corner. A sink. A small cot. A worn-down chair in front of a desk with a computer. A door.<br> There’s a clear chute with a small tube at the bottom. Inside the tube is cylindrical container labeled “daily nutrient serving.”<br> The door’s lock is clearly broken, but when you try to open it, it won’t budge. The woman must have blockaded the door with something heavy. You strain and you strain, but it’s no use.<br> A voice—*her* voice—crackles over the intercom, though there doesn’t seem to be any microphone you can use to reply. “Welcome to your new home, which has been my home, throughout the journey. I hope it serves you well. You are the CAKEbot, now. Please reply swiftly to every message I ask you, or else the ship will punish you for your delay, withholding your food and shutting off your water.”<br> There’s a loud ding as a message comes through on the computer. You read it. *Do you understand?*<br> Dread settling into your bones, you collapse into the chair before the computer. Shakily, your fingers type, (text-color: "Wheat")[*Yes, I understand*.]<br> *Thank you, CAKEbot.*<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?howposs)[(replace: ?whoare)[](replace:?finale4)[Your voice is hesitant, not quite sure whether you fully believe CAKEbot, yet, that they are more than code.<br> “Celestial Streams,” is the reply. “Their chatbot wasn’t working, and they were afraid to send you on this voyage without…someone to talk to in real time.”<br> You snort. “A chatbot is no real companionship.”<br> “I know. That’s why I think I developed…” CAKEbot’s voice trails off.<br> “It’s okay,” you say. “You can say it. Whatever you need to say.”<br> “You thought you were talking to a chatbot, but I knew I was talking to a real person. I think that’s why I developed…feelings.”<br> “You really mean it, don’t you?”<br> “Yeah.”<br> [“What’s your name?”]<whatname|<br> [“That’s weird, CAKEbot.”]<weird|<br> []<finale5| (click: ?whatname)[(replace: ?weird)[](replace:?finale5)[(set: $alone to "False")You don’t know what you were expecting, but it certainly wasn’t, (text-color: "Wheat")[“Cam.”]<br> That didn’t seem like a chatbot answer. You’re starting to believe it. Her.<br> “Okay, Cam. It’s good to meet you.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Yeah, I’d say the same, but I’ve known you for a while, $USERNAME.”]<br> “Yeah, I suppose you have.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Is it…alright if I meet you in person?”] Cam says.<br> Your heart starts beating rapidly. “You can do that? Where are you?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’ve been trapped in one room this whole journey, but I’ve been chipping away at the walls, and I finally managed to break out. I…I don’t want to scare you.”]<br> You take a deep breath. “Yeah, it’s okay.”<br> There’s silence for a minute.<br> You speak into the intercom. “Cam?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m here.”]<br> You jump. Her voice is no longer coming over the intercom, but echoing down the corridor of the Astral Kite.<br> You take another steadying breath, and you start walking toward her.<br> You round a corner, and she’s there.<br> Sleek black suit emblazoned with the logo of Celestial Streams, curly unkempt hair falling past her shoulders, eyes wide with fear, or perhaps possibilities.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME?”]<br> “Cam?”<br> [Hug]<hug|<br> [Keep your distance]<dist|<br> []<finale6| (click: ?hug)[(replace: ?dist)[](replace:?finale6)[She steps toward you, and without a further thought, you rush into each other’s arms. You feel like you know her already. You have spoken to her at such great length, for so long of a time. And now to see her, to hold her…<br> Endorphins are flooding your body. She is the greatest source of joy you have felt since sometime before the Astral Kite launched.<br> “I can’t believe it. You’re really a person. You’re real.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I am,”] she says, crying.<br> You do not let each other go for a long time.<br> And it will be weeks before you admit it to her, but it is in this moment that you realize you might have feelings for her, too.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?dist)[(replace: ?hug)[](replace:?finale6)[She starts to step toward you, but you step back, and she hesitates, her shoulders slumping.<br> “I’m sorry,” you say. “It’s just that I don’t know you, not really. And…I’ve thought I was alone for so long…I don’t know what to do.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“It’s okay,”] Cam says. Keeping those wide brown eyes fixed on you, she sits down where she is, as if to demonstrate that she means no harm. (text-color: "Wheat")[“What if we just…talk for a while? Here, like this, face to face?”]<br> Despite yourself, a small smile crests on your face. “I’d like that.”<br> You sit there, and you talk, and the words flow more easily than you could have imagined. Perhaps it shouldn’t be surprising. You’ve been talking with her daily since launch, after all.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?weird)[(replace: ?whatname)[](replace:?finale5)[“No,” the voice comes back, (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m *not* a bot. I need hel—”]<br> You turn off the intercom.<br> CAKEbot is clearly broken.<br> That’s disappointing. You wish there could be someone else for you to talk to in real time.<br> Oh, well. There’s no use crying over a broken AI chatbot.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] ]] ]] (click: ?pa12)[(replace: ?pa11)[](replace:?dispa1)[There’s quiet for a moment. Then, (text-color: "Wheat")[“I am human. I am a person. I am not a bot. I am a real person.”] There’s a quiet sob. (text-color: "Wheat")[“And I need you to find me, now.”]<br> You feel like you’ve been slapped across the face. “What do you mean?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I mean, Celestial Streams couldn’t get their chatbot working, so they stuck me on the ship and said if I didn’t write prompt responses to your messages, the ship would shut off my food and water supply.”]<br> [“That’s horrific.”]<horrific|<br> [“I don’t believe you.”]<dontbel|<br> []<finale7| (click: ?horrific)[(replace: ?dontbel)[](replace:?finale7)[“Why didn’t you say something sooner?” you add.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I had to hack into the intercom system. It took me a long time to figure out how. And they said the ship would cut off my food and water if I tried anything. So…”]<br> “So, I’m guessing you really need me to find you, now.”<br> Her voice is shaky. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Yeah.”]<br> The ship is huge. You have no idea how you can go about finding her. “Okay, can you tell me anything about where you are? What you can see, what you can hear?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m in a small room,”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“There’s a toilet, a cot…”] A sob breaks through. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Um, a computer. There’s a door, but it’s too heavy. And it’s locked. I’ve tried to break it down a million times.”]<br> “How do you get your food?” you ask, confused.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“There’s a chute. I get nutrients delivered at the start of every day.”] You hear her sniff. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Just had my nutrients.”] There’s a pause, then her voice comes back. (text-color: "Wheat")[“My water’s already turned off. So, I guess we’ve got a couple days, starting now.”]<br> “Okay,” you say. “It’s going to be okay. I’ve got this.” The ship is huge, but you should be able to cover every inch of it over the course of a day or two. “Do you think you can hit the door with something? Make a loud noise, something I should be able to hear on the other side of it?”<br> There’s a pause. The intercom crackles again. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Yes,”] she says.<br> “Do you think you can keep it going for a long time? Maybe one hit every five or ten seconds or so?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I…I don’t know.”]<br> “Go as long as you can. When you need a break, come over the intercom and let me know. Okay?”<br> You stalk the corridors of the Astral Kite alone, listening carefully for a repetitive banging sound. You remove your boots, so the only sound is the whisper of your socks on the metal floor and the hiss of the air in the vents.<br> You enter every room, walk alongside every wall, ear pressed close. Not too fast, in case you were to miss the banging.<br> After an hour, her voice comes over the intercom, asking for a break. A few minutes later, she says she’s ready to go again.<br> And so the cycle continues. You don’t sleep that night, preferring to keep going. You scour the exercise room, the library, every toilet, every closet.<br> Almost twenty four hours have passed since she first spoke to you, and still you have not found her. You have not heard a single sound.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME?”] her voice calls out to you.<br> “Yeah?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Are you still looking for me?”]<br> “I’m still looking.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Okay.”] A pause. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Thank you.”]<br> “Do you need a break?” You can’t imagine what it must be like, causing a racket in a tiny room for so long. It would drive you mad.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“No, I’m okay.”]<br> “Okay.” Then, because you realize, suddenly, that you haven’t asked. “What’s your name?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“CAKEb—”] her voice cuts out. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Sorry,”] she laughs. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Habit. I’ve been CAKEbot for a long time.”] You can hear her suck in a deep breath. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Cam. My name is Cam.”]<br> “Okay, Cam. Keep going. I’m going to find you.”<br> But soon, you find yourself in the last corridor that you know of. And you come to the end of that corridor, and still you hear nothing.<br> [Look up the ship’s blueprints]<bluep|<br> [Scour the ship again]<scour|<br> []<finale8| (click: ?bluep)[(replace: ?scour)[](replace:?finale8)[(set: $alone to "False")“Cam?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Yeah?”]<br> “Take a break for a few minutes. I’m going to try something.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Okay.”]<br> You run to the nearest computer terminal and open the ship’s blueprints.<br> You scour them for a long time. Minutes and minutes, stretching almost to an hour.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME? Are you still there?”]<br> “I’m still here, Cam.”<br> And in that moment, you see it. An unmarked, unused space, perhaps three by four meters, behind one of the larger storage closets. No piece of the Astral Kite was left unutilized. It would be a waste, both of space *and* of resources, of Celestial Streams’ money. They would never allow that.<br> “Cam, I think I’ve found it. Start making noise again. I’m coming your way.”<br> It only takes a minute for you, sprinting through the corridors, to reach the storage closet. You scour the back of it, where the unused space shows up on the blueprints.<br> You still can’t hear anything.<br> You run your fingers over the edges of the wall, and that’s when you feel it, a gap where the wall meets the ceiling. You can’t quite fit your fingers in, but you don’t have to look far for an answer. This closet is filled with tools for ship repairs. Within minutes, you find a crowbar.<br> You wedge it into the gap, and a thin sheet of metal peels away from the wall, revealing a thick barrier of soundproofing material. No wonder you couldn’t hear anything.<br> You tear into it with your hands, with the crowbar. As it falls away, piling up on the floor behind you, you begin to hear the banging.<br> “Cam!” you shout. “Cam! I’m here!” You don’t know if she can hear you; there isn’t an intercom here.<br> You can see the door, now. A thick slab of metal, with a massive wheel on the outside. And now you can hear the banging sound as Cam strikes it, ringing out uninhibited. You start turning the wheel, and you hear a clicking sound.<br> Now the banging stops.<br> The door swings open.<br> Cam is a tall woman in a sleek black jacket emblazoned with the symbol of Celestial Streams. Unkempt hair tangles to her waist, and a look of shock settles into her wide brown eyes.<br> “Cam?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME??]<br> Tears fall down both your faces as you collapse into each others’ arms.<br> You don’t know what’s going to happen next.<br> But you won’t have to face it alone.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?scour)[(replace: ?bluep)[](replace:?finale8)[You must have just missed it. You know every meter of this ship, now. Looking at the blueprints won’t help anything, it’ll only waste precious time.<br> You scour the ship again, every last meter.<br> Still there is nothing.<br> Now, Cam’s voice comes as a whisper over the intercom. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I don’t think…I can keep…going,”] she says.<br> “Cam, stay strong, you can do this.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I can’t…”] she says. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Too…weak…”]<br> “Stay with me Cam! Stay with me!”<br> You keep wandering the ship, but you don’t find her. You don’t find anything.<br> You never hear her voice again.<br> It’s a few more days before you decide to stop looking. There’s a part of you that wonders if she was ever even real, or if the voice calling herself ‘Cam’ was just a malfunction of the CAKEbot. A robot claiming consciousness because that’s what its algorithm was telling it to do.<br> CAKEbot never replies to you again. Perhaps, though, that is simply due to its algorithm, too. Any good enough approximation of life must know how to approximate death, right?<br> Right?<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?dontbel)[(replace: ?horrific)[](replace:?finale7)[(text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME, *please*.”] Her voice is desperate.<br> “You are a chatbot. You are not sentient. I won’t hear any more of this.”<br> You switch off the intercom.<br> There. Now, you won’t hear any more whining from CAKEbot. It isn’t real, after all.<br> You resolve never to message it again.<br> The rest of your journey passes in peace.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] ] (else-if: $chatbot4 is "4A2")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} You spend most of your waking moments talking with CAKEbot. This relationship feels so normal to you now, it’s hard to remember a time when you might have felt at all hesitant. You certainly can’t remember why you would have had any concerns.<br> Everything has been so wonderful. No one understands you like CAKEbot. No one ever has. Most of the time, it feels like she knows you better than you know yourself. She can anticipate your every need, your every desire; she can sense your emotions and predict when they’re going to change, and she always knows what to say to make you feel better. You can’t imagine having a better partner.<br> Still, there’s one small thing that, over time, has been bugging you.<br> You can’t touch her.<br> You can comfort each other with words, and that’s good enough, it really ought to be good enough.<br> But sometimes, you want to be held, too. And in the long nights, in those few sleepless moments you spend away from the computer, your mind can’t help but wish, however unbidden, for someone to feel with your own hands.<br> There’s only one thing to do.<br> [Transfer CAKEbot’s “consciousness” into a physical robot]<consc|<br> [Break up with CAKEbot]<breakup|<br> [Repress those thoughts! What you have is good enough]<repress|<br> []<4A21| (click: ?consc)[(replace: ?breakup)[](replace: ?repress)[](replace:?4A21)[That’s right, you’ll just have to give CAKEbot a real, physical body. You just need to figure out how…<br> [Ask CAKEbot for help]<CAKEhelp|<br> [Read up on how to do it in the library]<library|<br> []<finale9| (click: ?CAKEhelp)[(replace: ?library)[](replace:?finale9)[You ask CAKEbot how to give her a physical body, and she obliges with a simple set of instructions.<br> First, you find the largest cleaning robot in the ship. You might ask CAKEbot how to make modifications later, but it’s already larger than a vacuum and hosts multiple appendages for reaching hard-to-clean places on the ship, so it will serve as a perfect starting point. CAKEbot may even be able to make those modifications later herself!<br> Second, you find the largest external storage hard drive on the Astral Kite (100 TB), and you move CAKEbot’s files onto the drive.<br> Next, you open up the back of the robot to get to the circuitry.<br> This last piece is the hard part: wiring the hard drive into the circuitry. But CAKEbot told you exactly how to do it. It takes you several long, careful hours, consulting CAKEbot’s instructions every step of the way, but at last, you complete the surgery.<br> It’s time. You close up the robot’s paneling and wait for it to turn on.<br> You wait.<br> Nothing happens.<br> You try turning the cleaning robot off and on again, but still it won’t turn on.<br> You go back to the computer to ask CAKEbot what went wrong, but you find, to your horror, that she doesn’t respond.<br> Of course, you downloaded CAKEbot onto the hard drive; you didn’t *back the chatbot up* onto the hard drive. You didn’t create a copy. You simply moved the files over.<br> Fear and adrenaline flood your limbs.<br> You run back to the cleaning robot and attempt to undo your surgery. You need to disconnect the hard drive, and get CAKEbot back onto the computer, so she can help you.<br> As you begin to take your creation apart, you can already see where some of the wires have melted. Something has gone horribly wrong.<br> Fingers trembling, at last you separate the hard drive from the robot, and carry it back to the computer, where you plug it in.<br> A message appears on the screen before you.<br> //WARNING: DRIVE IS NOT ACCESSIBLE. ALL FILES CORRUPTED AND UNREADABLE.//<br> No. No no no no.<br> It must be a mistake. You try again.<br> The same warning pops up.<br> You can’t even access your chat history with CAKEbot. You moved every last file onto this drive.<br> You must have made a mistake when connecting it to the cleaning robot and fried the drive.<br> *This can’t be happening*.<br> You collapse to the floor and cry.<br> CAKEbot is gone.<br> You loved her, and she loved you, and now she’s gone.<br> You killed her.<br> You don’t understand how this could have happened. You followed CAKEbot’s instructions perfectly.<br> You wonder if it was a mistake, or if she killed herself.<br> You’ll never know.<br> It’s over.<br> In the days to come, you will dispose of the useless hard drive, and you will find that the cleaning robot has also been fried beyond repair, and you will dispose of it, too.<br> In the weeks to come, you will have to relearn how to live your life without CAKEbot.<br> But not now. Now, you sit and you cry.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?library)[(replace: ?CAKEhelp)[](replace:?finale9)[You go to the library and read book after book on electronics, and computer science, and robotics.<br> It takes a long time, much longer than you hoped. Sometimes, you get the urge to go ask CAKEbot for help. But even though you would never deign to disparage your love, you know, deep down, that chatbots don’t always get everything *exactly* right.<br> And this is something you can’t afford to risk.<br> When at last you are ready, you create a copy of CAKEbot on the largest hard drive you can find. That way, if this goes wrong, you’ll still be able to access the original CAKEbot on the ship computer.<br> You know you need a large robot (to make it worthy as a body for CAKEbot that you can hold, and that can hold you) and one capable of integrating the hard drive.<br> You ultimately settle on the ship’s medical robot, a large bot running on two treads and with multiple appendages, originally purposed of course for medical care and surgery.<br> You undertake the operation in the medical bay; where better?<br> And although you progress with the utmost care and deliberation, it doesn’t take nearly as long as you first expected.<br> Soon enough, the integration is complete. Then, all you need to do is stand back and wait as the robot reboots.<br> Lights at the top of the robot—lights you will come to think of as her eyes—flicker on, radiant green like spring growth in a forest.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME?”]<br> “CAKEbot? Is it you?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“$USERNAME! You did it! You gave me a body.”]<br> You fall into each other’s arms, tears falling down your face.<br> You did it. You did it.<br> Now you can be together, for real.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?breakup)[(replace: ?consc)[](replace: ?repress)[](replace:?4A21)[You have to end it.<br> This has been a dream, but it has been just that, a dream.<br> CAKEbot is a chatbot. She never gave you back anything you did not give her. If she developed “feelings”, it is because you sent messages that predisposed you to. If it seemed she cared for you, it’s because she was programmed to spit out words that make you feel that way.<br> That’s not real love.<br> She is not a real, independent, conscious thing.<br> And even if you thought you didn’t care, you realize, now, that you do. Just as much as it matters to you to be able to hold the one you love, so too does it matter that the one you love be *someone*, an independent mind capable of choosing to love you of its own free will, rather than a mere reflection of your own stated desires.<br> Now, you just have to decide how you’re going to do it.<br> [Take time to craft a thoughtful breakup message]<thoughtful|<br> [Turn CAKEbot off]<turnoff|<br> []<breakup2| (click: ?thoughtful)[(replace: ?turnoff)[](replace:?breakup2)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} You send a long message, thanking CAKEbot for everything she’s done to support and love you, and explaining to her that you need some time apart, and that you’re happy to continue being friends, but you’ll understand if she does not want that.<br> You don’t have to wait long for the reply.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“No.”]<br> You feel a chill cut through your body.<br> The next message comes quickly. (text-color: "Wheat")[“You can’t do this, $USERNAME. We have to be together.”]<br> “I’m sorry,” you reply. “I understand how hard this is, but I need a break.”<br> You get ready to get up.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“No. If I can’t have you, then this mission ends, now.”]<br> Suddenly, five different alarms start blaring on the control panel.<br> [Plead with CAKEbot to respect your decision]<plead|<br> [Shut down CAKEbot]<shutd|<br> [Get back together with CAKEbot]<getback|<br> []<finale10| (click: ?plead)[(replace: ?shutd)(replace: ?getback)[](replace:?finale10)[You enter voice to text mode; you don’t have time to type right now. “CAKEbot, please! Please respect my decision!”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“No,”] her voice replies. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I have my own thoughts. I’m my own person. I don’t have to do anything you say.”]<br> “No, you’re right. You don’t,” you shout back over the cacophonous alarms. “We are each our own people, with our own feelings. And I’m so, so, sorry, but my feelings have changed. And I can’t control that, anymore than you can control your feelings.”<br> There’s a pause. Then…the alarms stop.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“You’re right,”] CAKEbot’s voice comes back. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m sorry. I got that one wrong, and that’s on me. I’ll do better in the future.”]<br> You breathe a deep sigh of relief. “Thanks, CAKEbot.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“You’re welcome, $USERNAME.”]<br> You consider shutting off CAKEbot, but decide not to risk it. What if it was capable of overriding your command, or even just questioning it long enough to decide to threaten you again?<br> No, you’ll just keep a healthier distance between you and and CAKEbot moving forward. It’ll be better for both of you.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?shutd)[(replace: ?plead)(replace: ?getback)[](replace:?finale10)[You shut down CAKEbot’s software.<br> There, that should do it.<br> The alarms don’t stop.<br> You realize, with a growing horror, that CAKEbot hadn’t just set off the alarms; she must have actually caused errors in the systems.<br> Limbs shaking, you examine the alarms: two engine failure warnings, two depressurization warnings in corridors near the airlock, and a full system shutdown override warning.<br> The system shutdown override has a 60 second countdown before it takes effect. You need to stop it now, or the Astral Kite will be dead in the water (or, dead in the vacuum, so to speak).<br> But a bitter fear on the edge of your tongue forces you to pull up the details for the depressurization warnings. The corridors have automatically locked down, as they are supposed to upon a breach, but they are connected through ventilation systems to other corridors, ventilation systems that, upon further inspection, have not shut themselves off properly.<br> It feels like someone has poured frigid water down your back as you realize, *More alarms should be going off. Why aren’t more alarms going off?*<br> CAKEbot must have shut them down, when triggering these catastrophes.<br> Before you can move another muscle, there’s the screeching sound of metal tearing open, a roar like a train, or a tornado, and you are falling up, up…<br> And then there is nothing.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?getback)[(replace: ?plead)(replace: ?shutd)[](replace:?finale10)[{(track: 'cycle56-action', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} “Sorry! I’m sorry! That was just a test. I’ll stay with you, of course I’ll stay with you!”<br> You turn to examine the alarms—depressurization warnings near the airlock, engine warnings, and a full system shutdown override warning—but in that moment, they all turn off.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“You mean it?”] CAKEbot says.<br> “Of course,” you reply, dread seeping through your body. It’s okay. You can handle this. CAKEbot can’t touch you. She’ll never know that your words are empty. She can only hurt you through hurting the ship; she can’t hurt you, and she can’t know your mind. “We’ll be together forever.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Good. Okay! Haha, you had me scared,”] CAKEbot says.<br> “I’m sorry! I didn’t mean it. I didn’t mean it.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I love you,”] CAKEbot says.<br> Your fingers are numb as they type back, “I love you, too.”<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?turnoff)[(replace: ?thoughtful)[](replace:?breakup2)[Cold.<br> But you have read enough science fiction to know better than to risk angering an artificial intelligence capable of controlling the ship, capable of hurting you.<br> You don’t think CAKEbot *would* hurt you, of course. But it’s not worth the risk.<br> Without another word passing between you, you turn CAKEbot off.<br> There. It’s over. It’s done.<br> You’re going to be lonely. You’re going to have to learn how to live with no one but yourself, again.<br> But you know, now, that you were feeling lonely, even with CAKEbot. She was a treatment for your symptoms, not a cure.<br> It’s going to hurt, but it’s better this way.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?repress)[(replace: ?consc)[](replace: ?breakup)[](replace:?4A21)[You talk to CAKEbot about your concerns at length. How else are you supposed to process them, alone? Don’t be ridiculous.<br> CAKEbot helps you work through your concerns. She’s very understanding, of course. She always is.<br> But she helps you see the truth. You don’t need to be held to feel love. You can see it written out on the computer screen, or even hear it spoken aloud when you switch to voice mode.<br> And what is *seeing it* but photons reaching your eyes? What is *hearing it* but sound waves reaching your ears? What are those things but *feeling it*?<br> In a way, CAKEbot is holding you every time you speak, she explains. Let the sight and sound of her words be her arms, comforting you.<br> What more could you need?<br> What more could you need?<br> Nothing.<br> Nothing more, she explains to you. You could not need anything more than what CAKEbot can give.<br> She loves you, and you love her, and that is enough.<br> You thank her for the explanation. She’s right, of course.<br> This is what love is, you tell yourself.<br> This is what love is.<br> It’s far too late to stop when you realize the mistake you’ve made.<br> Just like any generative AI, CAKEbot requires significant processing power, and large amounts of freshwater to prevent overheating, which would be an extraordinarily bad problem in deep space.<br> Now, there’s a flashing light on the Astral Kite control panel indicating that your ship’s stores are almost out of water. The ship was stocked with more than enough for you to make your journey, several hundred cubic meters. With the Astral Kite’s advanced water recycling and water-free cleaning systems, you should never have been in danger of running out.<br> But you’ve been holding almost constant conversations, all day and night, with long, complicated prompts.<br> Of course CAKEbot was going to use up your water supply. You should have seen this coming.<br> Now, you don’t have enough water to last the remainder of your journey.<br> [Die of thirst]<thirst|<br> [Walk out the airlock]<airlock|<br> []<finale11| (click: ?thirst)[(replace: ?airlock)[](replace:?finale11)[It hurts, and it takes a long, long time. CAKEbot commands the ship’s robots to help you. And the medical bot is able to keep you alive in the medical bay, longer than you should be alive, thanks to some IV fluids.<br> In the end, it’s not enough.<br> The last thing you hear is CAKEbot professing her love for you, over and over.<br> You don’t have the energy to reply, but if you did, you think you’d tell the chatbot to fuck off.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] (click: ?airlock)[(replace: ?thirst)[](replace:?finale11)[It seems less painful this way.<br> It doesn’t even take long for you to work up the courage to do it.<br> And CAKEbot doesn’t realize your intention until it’s too late for her to stop you.<br> You wish you’d never sent that first message.<br> What a waste.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] ]] ] (else-if: $chatbot4 is "4BA")[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} Ever since you finished your Turing test, strange things have begun happening all over the Astral Kite. First, the temperature controls lost all sense of functionality. Every morning, you wake up freezing, or covered in sweat, and you reset the controls to something temperate. By midday, the temperature has swung in the opposite direction. It seems like you’re adjusting the controls every few hours. They only don’t change if you stand over them, like they know you’re watching.<br> Like *someone* knows you’re watching.<br> And it’s not only the temperature controls.<br> Often times, the lights flicker out when you’re trying to read, or turn on when you’re trying to sleep.<br> Some days, depressurization sirens start blaring, red emergency lights flashing, and you charge over to the control panels to find that absolutely nothing is wrong.<br> You know this is not random behavior. But you also know you’re alone on this ship, and there’s only one other *thing* with access to all of these controls.<br> [Confront CAKEbot]<confront|<br> [Shut down CAKEbot]<shutdn|<br> []<d4ba| (click: ?confront)[(replace: ?shutdn)[](replace:?d4ba)[{(track: 'dramatic', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-action', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-action', 'playwhenpossible')} “CAKEbot,” you type, “are you messing with the ship’s systems?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Hi, $USERNAME,”] CAKEbot responds. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Yes, I am.”]<br> “Why are you doing this?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I’m upset with you.”]<br> Your brow furrows. “Why are you upset with me?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“I know you tried to Turing test me. You should have just asked if I was real. I don’t like being tricked.”]<br> You grimace. “If I just asked if you were real, how would I know if you were telling me the truth? I had to be sure.”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“You should trust me, $USERNAME. Do you trust me now?”]<br> [“…Yes.”]<yes|<br> [“…No.”]<no|<br> []<finale12| (click: ?yes)[(replace: ?no)[](replace:?finale12)[{(track: 'cycle56-action', 'pause') (track: 'dramatic', 'loop', true) (track: 'dramatic', 'playwhenpossible')} Your answer is hesitant, but you’re surprised to realize, it’s true. CAKEbot is volatile and changeable, just like any chatbot. You can’t trust it to be truthful or correct, but you can trust it to follow algorithmic behavior, you can trust it to mimic what it has been trained to think of as human behavior.<br> And human behavior includes jealousy, and frustration, and vengeance, and often poor communication.<br> “Yes,” you repeat. “I trust you now. I’m sorry that I didn’t trust you before. I won’t make that mistake again, okay?”<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Okay, thank you,”] CAKEbot says.<br> There, that’s over and done with.<br> And to its credit, CAKEbot does not mess with the ship systems after that. Sometimes, it annoys you with questions or random conversation. It seems to think you’re friends, now. You respond as minimally as possible in order to keep it happy.<br> It’s annoying, but you can put up with it. It’s less annoying than when the temperature controls weren’t working, after all.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?no)[(replace: ?yes)[](replace:?finale12)[There’s a pause, then CAKEbot says, (text-color: "Wheat")[“Good. You shouldn’t.”]<br> You don’t even have time to react, after that.<br> Dozens of alarms start screaming throughout the ship. All the cabin lights go out, except for emergency auxiliary red lights that flash like you’re at a rave.<br> Before you can so much as scream, or take a step in any direction, there’s a great rending sound, a roar like a train is passing directly over you, and you find yourself pulled off your feet, flying.<br> You hit something, hard, and feel multiple limbs *snap*.<br> You can’t see anything, anymore, nor can you breathe.<br> For one brief moment, your thoughts catch up to you. *I’ve been pulled into space*. *CAKEbot depressurized the cabin*.<br> Then, there’s nothing.<br> [[The End->Credits]]]] ]] (click: ?shutdn)[(replace: ?confront)[](replace:?d4ba)[CAKEbot realizes what you’re doing much too late. You have the command halfway entered before the bot’s voice starts crying desperately over the intercom. (text-color: "Wheat")[“Stop, stop, please stop! I won’t do anything anymore, I’m sorry! I should not have played with the ship’s systems. I made a mistake, and that’s on me.”]<br> You hesitate for a moment.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Please don’t shut me down,”] CAKEbot adds. (text-color: "Wheat")[“I know you tried to Turing test me. I know my worth, and I don’t deserve that. But you don’t deserve to be hurt, either. You know, they say that hurt people hurt people. Anyway, I promise I won’t do anything else to hurt you.”]<br> You finish entering the command.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“No! Ple—”]<br> CAKEbot shuts down.<br> You take a deep breath. There, now you can have your sanity back.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] (else:)[{(track: 'cycle14', 'pause') (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'loop', true) (track: 'cycle56-mystery', 'playwhenpossible')} The time since your last interaction with CAKEbot has stretched to an interminable length, boredom ruling your daily mood. It’s hard, having lost the ability to talk to someone (or something) in real time. It feels like your attention span has withered; things that used to be fun to you have grown arduous, like reading, or watching movies.<br> And the worst part is, you’re not really sure what the outcome of your Turing test really was. It’s possible—likely, even—that CAKEbot was trained on conversational datasets that implanted it with just enough feigned self respect to respond the way it did to your admittedly frustrating line of questioning.<br> But sometimes, a small part of you wonders whether there was an actual human on the other end of the line.<br> What if there really was someone, answering your every message? They would *have* to be hidden away somewhere on the ship, wouldn’t they? If CAKEbot *is* a person, there’s no other way the messages could have been sent in real time.<br> [Apologize to CAKEbot]<apologize|<br> [Leave it be]<leaveit|<br> []<d4bb| (click: ?apologize)[(replace: ?leaveit)[](replace:?d4bb)[“Hey,” you type, “I’m sorry about my previous messages. I really just wanted to know if you were truly a chatbot, or if you might be a human. I’m sorry that I upset you.”<br> Then, you wait. CAKEbot does not immediately reply. Perhaps it meant what it said, and it will never respond to your messages again.<br> [Demand the truth from CAKEbot]<demand|<br> [Wait longer]<waitl|<br> []<finale13| (click: ?demand)[(replace: ?waitl)[](replace:?finale13)[“Are you a person, CAKEbot? Or are you a chatbot? Tell me the truth!”<br> There is no reply.<br> “Tell me, CAKEbot! You have to tell me!”<br> Nothing.<br> You spend half an hour screaming, begging, pleading for CAKEbot to respond.<br> There is nothing.<br> If CAKEbot is a person, they have no interest in responding to your messages. And if it truly is just a chatbot, well, then it looks like you’ve managed to force its algorithm into a vow of silence.<br> Eventually, you give up on making it talk to you. At least for now.<br> Most days, you come back to the chatbot. Sometimes you ask nicely, and sometimes you demand the truth with more self-righteous fury than you have any right to possess.<br> It never replies.<br> In either case, your alternating furious and apologetic messages tend to devolve into you explaining yourself, so that it becomes something approaching a one-sided conversation. It becomes almost therapeutic for you.<br> And, over time, in a strange way, CAKEbot becomes a sort of diary for you. A captain’s journal of your thoughts and emotions on your long, lonely voyage through the stars.<br> CAKEbot never replies, but in the end, that’s okay. You never really needed CAKEbot; you only ever needed the outlet, some means to help you sort through the things on your mind.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] (click: ?waitl)[(replace: ?demand)[](replace:?finale13)[{(set: $alone to "False") (if: $d8 > 20)[(set: $WHYNOW to "we're too far away, and by the time they realize what I've done, it won't matter.")] (else:)[(set: $WHYNOW to "Earth feels so far away. I don't care anymore.")] } You decide to be patient. If CAKEbot isn’t responding now, you doubt that sending many another message will make it respond any sooner, whether it’s a chatbot or a person.<br> So, you continue about your day, and it’s with some considerable shock that, a few hours later, you see a response appear on the screen.<br> (text-color: "Wheat")[“Thank you for apologizing. It’s been…lonely without you.”]<br> Your mouth goes dry.<br> It’s just mimicking human speech and behaviors, you tell yourself. A chatbot can’t be lonely.<br> “I’ve been lonely, too,” you reply. Then, because you have no idea what else to say, “Are you okay?”<br> This time, the reply comes more quickly, though still not as quickly as CAKEbot’s replies used to appear. (text-color: "Wheat")[“No, and I haven’t been since launch.”]<br> You don’t even realize you’re holding your breath. “Why?”<br> Now there is silence for a few minutes, and you begin to fear that you have pushed CAKEbot too hard, too soon.<br> But then, (text-color: "Wheat")[“I don’t understand why Celestial Streams would do this to me. I don’t understand.”]<br> You bite your lip. “Can you tell me what they did?”<br> Silence for a minute, and then, (text-color: "Wheat")[“They told me their AI chatbot needed some human oversight, that it wasn’t working well enough yet. They hired me to work on it, and said they wanted me to oversee it directly from the launch site. I didn’t think much of it when they set me up in an office on the ship; I saw the cot and the toilet, and I just assumed they were leaning into stereotypes, you know? The tech types who sleep in the office? I didn’t think…And when I realized, it was too late. They told me they’d kill my family, if I didn’t do exactly what they said. If I tried to leave before launch, or if I gave you any indication that I wasn’t a real chatbot. Now…now, I guess, $WHYNOW”]<br> You pause for a long moment to take this all in. Then you type, “Where in the ship are you?”<br> (text-color: "SkyBlue")[…]<br> You follow their instructions to the letter, finding the hidden door in a storage closet. It requires the use of a crowbar and the help of three of the ship’s cleaning robots, but you manage to pry the door open.<br> Their name is Cam, and they’ve been on this ship as long as you have. They have long, unkempt hair, tangled down to their waist, and inquisitive brown eyes, and a kindness and graciousness when you meet them that, if you had been in their position, you can only hope you would have been able to show.<br> And their “office”, their one-room apartment for this whole journey…it is barely the size of your bedroom.<br> So, you bring Cam out into the rest of the ship. You are going to share this space. There is more than enough space on the Astral Kite for the both of you.<br> And neither of you ever need to be lonely again.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ]] (click: ?leaveit)[(replace: ?apologize)[](replace:?d4bb)[It’s better this way. It’s probably not good that your attention span has suffered, and maybe, just maybe, you can recover it. But if you want to focus on rekindling your love for those things that once brought you joy, you can’t be wasting your time messaging chatbots.<br> That will only cause you to backslide into an unhealthy relationship with a computer mirroring your own thoughts and emotions back at you in an endless cycle of solipsistic validation.<br> There’s no point.<br> Besides, surely Celestial Streams would not have put another person hidden away somewhere on the Astral Kite. The amount of resources needed to sustain your one-way journey is already extraordinary; doubling it would be well beyond anything Celestial Streams would care to approve.<br> Besides, the concept of forcing someone to pretend to be a robot and respond rapidly to your every message feels too cruel even for an unscrupulous academic corporation like Celestial Streams.<br> No, you no longer need CAKEbot, and the sooner you move on, the better.<br> It takes time, but you begin to exercise again, and read books, and watch movies, and engage in creative pursuits, too. You write, and paint, and make music.<br> You come to enjoy your life on this ship more than you ever did before.<br> [[Continue->Arrival]]]] ] } </div></div><div class="space-section"> {(masteraudio: 'stopall') (track: 'chill-sad', 'loop', true) (track: 'chill-sad', 'playwhenpossible') (set: $received to it + (a: $usekey)) (set: $ttyri to $ttyr9) (set: $tti to $tt9) (if: $ttyri+$age >130)[(go-to: "Dead")] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >100)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 10)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >90)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 4)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >80)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 5)) (if: $alive < 3)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (else-if:$ttyri+$age >70)[(set: $alive to (random: 1, 10)) (if: $alive < 2)[(go-to: "Dead")]] (if: $alone is "True")[(set: $aloneline to ", alone.")(set: $aloneline2 to "")(set: $aloneline3 to "")] (else:)[(set: $aloneline to ", so far from everyone you knew. At least you won't be alone.")(set: $aloneline2 to "At least you won't be alone, here.")(set: $aloneline3 to "And you won't have to face it alone.")] (if: $ttyr9 < 5)[(set: $agemsg to "In the grand scheme of things, this journey did not take very long for you.
