But this was planned as a one-way journey, and even if you were to return to Earth, things will have changed so much since you’ve gone. Now, you’re forced to ponder the reality of spending the rest of your life out here") + $aloneline] (else-if: $ttyr9 < 15)[(set: $agemsg to "Though much less time has passed for you than everyone back home, it has still been years since you set out on your journey. Perhaps it’s the solitude, but you feel much older. You wonder how things would have gone for you if you'd never joined the Scholars Draft. " + $aloneline2)] (else-if: $ttyr9 < 30)[(set: $agemsg to "You’ve been traveling for many years, and the extended time in space has taken its toll on your body. You feel older and much more tired. You wonder how much of it is normal wear and tear on your body. Would you feel this way if you had stayed on Earth, if you'd never joined the Scholars draft? You’ll never know. " + $aloneline2)] (else:)[(set: $agemsg to "You’ve been traveling for decades. It feels strange, now, to have reached your destination, like you didn’t quite believe you’d ever make it. Stranger, though, is the realization that you don’t entirely remember what Earth was like. Not really. Of course, this was planned as a one-way journey, but you doubt you would live long enough to survive the journey back, anyway. And things have changed so much. Earth could never really be a home to you, anymore. You look back outside the viewport. This seems like a fine place to pass the remainder of your time. " + $aloneline3)] } {(live: 1.5s)[(stop:)(transition: "dissolve")[<div class="text-overlay"> (if: "6D" is in $received or "8A" is in $received)[$t9 have passed on Earth since your departure. $tt9 have passed on the Astral Kite since your departure.<br> Your spacecraft decelerates as it approaches your destination.<br> (link-replace: "Look out the viewport")[(show: ?ending)]] (else:)[$incoming<br> This message was sent from Earth $me_sent9 after launch, and it has reached the ship $t9 after launch. Only $tt9 have passed for you on this ship.<br> (link-replace: "Open message")[(show: ?earth1)] |earth1)[(text-color: "LightPink")[Professor $USERNAME, this is our final message to you on your journey. If we have timed this correctly, you should be receiving this message as you arrive at your destination. It has been our honor to serve as your stewards on this groundbreaking voyage.]<br> (link-replace: "Look out the viewport")[(show: ?ending)] ]] |ending)[ (if: $DESTINATION contains "M57")[Your spacecraft comes to a stop about a quarter of a light year from the planetary nebula known as M57, or the Ring Nebula. The name “planetary nebula,” of course, is an old misnomer; it has nothing to do with planets. When stars less than 8 times the mass of the Sun run out of Hydrogen to fuse into Helium, they no longer radiate enough energy to support their incredible mass, causing their cores to contract into dense white dwarfs while their atmosphere is ejected into space. The beautiful shells of ionized gas fill the sky before you in the shape of an eye, stretching more than 2 light years across, a little less bright than a full moon. The outer edges are flared in fiery crimson tendrils extending from a sharp, bright, tangerine ring. The clouds fades through yellow and sclera-like white to a pale aloe green that surrounds a brilliant azure blue iris. Dark knots of dense gas speckle the nebula. And at the very center of it all is small, bright, white dwarf star, the remains of the star that produced the planetary nebula. It’s beautiful.<br> $agemsg<br>] (else-if: $DESTINATION contains "PSR")[Your Celestial Streams spacecraft settles into orbit around this system, which actually contains a binary—both the millisecond pulsar that brought you here, and a companion white dwarf star. However, to the naked eye, this system is not the most interesting. As a millisecond pulsar, PSR J0437-4715 is spinning incredibly rapidly, 5750 rotations per second, with a stability to the rotation that’s as reliable as an atomic clock. The pulsar is also beaming out powerful emission in the X-ray wavelengths. Unfortunately, none of this is visible in the optical wavelengths. All you can see are the two small, white stars orbiting each other. At least this system will be exciting to study with all the instruments on board your craft.<br> $agemsg<br>] (else-if: $DESTINATION contains "TOI-700")[In the TOI-700 star system, your Celestial Streams spacecraft arrives in orbit around the fourth exoplanet in the system, TOI-700d. TOI-700 is a red dwarf star, smaller, redder, and dimmer than the Sun. TOI-700d is orbiting at a distance of about one sixth of an Astronomical Unit—one sixth the average Earth-Sun distance—within its star’s expected habitable zone, where liquid water should be able to exist. The world is almost exactly the same size as Earth, with almost twice the Earth’s mass. Now that you’re here, you’ll be able to explore the planet in much more detail. Does it have an atmosphere? Does it have oceans? Does it host life? You head to the viewport of your spacecraft, excited to look at this exciting world, something no one else has ever done.<br> $agemsg<br>] (else-if: $DESTINATION contains "J318")[Your Celestial Streams spacecraft enters orbit around the rogue planet PSO J318.5−22. The world itself is a gas giant, larger than Jupiter, and there has been debate among astronomers about whether it’s actually a true planet, or more accurately a brown dwarf—a failed star that never achieved nuclear fusion. Even now that you are here, you won’t be able to tell be eye; you’ll need to make some observations to determine the truth of the matter. But whether it is a true rogue planet or not, it is strange to find yourself orbiting a world with no true star in sight. As a gas giant, there’s no surface on the world for you to visit. But the world is beautiful nonetheless; hued in reds, whites, and yellows and covered in clouds.<br> $agemsg<br>] (else-if: $DESTINATION contains "Proxima")[Your Celestial Streams spacecraft enters its orbit at one tenth of an Astronomical Unit—one tenth of the average Earth-Sun distance—from Proxima Centauri, one of three stars in the Alpha Centauri star system. Although the other two stars are Sun-like G and K-type stars, Proxima Centauri is a red dwarf star, smaller, redder, and dimmer than the Sun. Despite this, those companion stars are about 13,000 Astronomical Units away, making them too dim to see, due to your proximity to Proxima Centauri. Perhaps more excitingly, there is at least one exoplanet known to orbit Proxima Centauri, a roughly Earth-sized world in the theoretical habitable zone around the star, where liquid water should be able to exist. Now that you’re here, there’s a lot for you to study. And if the conditions allow it, maybe you’ll one day explore that world.<br> $agemsg<br>] [[The End->Credits]] ] </div>]]}</div><div class="space-section"><div class="text-overlay"> {(masteraudio: 'stopall') (track: 'chill-sad', 'loop', true) (track: 'chill-sad', 'playwhenpossible')} $tti have passed for you since you set out on your journey. Unfortunately, you did not choose your velocity and destination wisely. You have grown old, and your health is failing you. Despite the best efforts of the medical bots on the Astral Kite, you're out of time, and you know it. The Astral Kite will arrive at $dest one day, but you won't be alive to see it. Now, lying in your bed in the spacecraft living quarters, out of sight of the viewport and the extraordinary Universe beyond, your thoughts stray back to your time on Earth. And in your final days, when you dream, you dream that you never left at all. You dream of the golden warmth of real sunlight on your face, of the laughter of friends around a dinner table, of the chirping of birds and the whispering of trees' leaves in a gentle breeze. When your eyes close for the final time, a soft smile lingers on your face. [[The End->Credits]] </div></div>(set: $food to "spicy") (set: $music to "rap") (set: $regrets to "no ragrets") (set: $USERNAME to "Jonathan") (set: $p2 to "their") (set: $dest to "M57") [[Credits]]
